title: Mapping of Habitat Suitability for Endemic and Vulnerable Flora in Caraga Region using Ensemble Species Distribution Modelling
authors: Richielyn May G. Soso, Dominique D. Magpatoc, Arturo G. Cauba, Romell A. Seronay
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Shorea contorta and Shorea negrosensis are Philippine endemic species classified as vulnerable under DAO 2017-11. This study modeled their habitat suitability and potential distribution in the Caraga Region under current climate conditions using ensemble species distribution modeling. Georeferenced presence points (37 for S. contorta and 79 for S. negrosensis) and 29 initial predictor variables were used in two approaches: (1) environmental variables only and (2) both environmental and anthropogenic variables. The models showed excellent performance, with AUC and TSS scores of $\mathbf{1. 0}$ for $\mathbf{S}$. contorta across both approaches, and 0.97 and 0.86 (approach 1) and 0.98 and 0.88 (approach 2) for S. negrosensis. The results indicate that both species exhibit distinct habitat suitability patterns with differing proportions of highly, moderately, lowly, and not suitable, influenced by anthropogenic factors with a decreasing trend of highly suitable areas as anthropogenic variables are included, highlighting the detrimental effect of human-induced factors.

title: TID Impact on 30nm NC FinFET with PZT oxide
authors: Suman Lata Tripathi, Neeraj Nayan Prakash, Balwinder Raj
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: This paper presents a detailed work on the influence of Total Ionizing Dose (TID) radiation on the electrical behaviour of both conventional FinFET and Negative Capacitance FinFET structures. The NC FinFET configuration integrates a ferroelectric PZT (lead zirconate titanate) layer while benchmark comparisons were carried out using SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> and HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> as standard gate oxides. All simulations were executed using advanced 3D TCAD environments to capture the degradation in essential device metric including threshold voltage, subthreshold slope (SS) and off-state leakage current under increasing radiation exposure. The simulated structures were defined with a gate length of 30nm fin width of 10nm fin height of 70nm and oxide thickness of 3nm. Precise doping concentrations were implemented with 2 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup> cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> in the source/drain regions and 1 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">16</sup> cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> in the channel to replicate practical device behaviour. This analysis emphasizes the importance of material selection and structural tuning to enhance device resilience under ionizing radiation. The incorporation of PZT in NC FinFET is shown to improve stability against radiation-induced defects due to its inherent polarization characteristics, effectively reducing charge trapping and threshold voltage shifts compared to FinFET with conventional high-k dielectrics.

title: Versatile Place and Route with Continuous Routing Runtime Prediction and Smart Route Termination
authors: Andrew David Gunter, Steven J E Wilton
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) routing is computationally expensive, taking hours or days with no guarantee of success. While prior work has used machine learning (ML) to guide placement and routing or predict routing outcomes, the process remains challenging to model precisely. This demo presents a new machine learning-based tool that addresses this problem.

title: Wideband Digital Post-Distortion for Improved Power-Efficiency and Coverage in OFDMA Networks
authors: Huseyin Babaroglu, Lauri Anttila, Oskari Tervo, Esa Tiirola, Mikko Valkama
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: This paper introduces a novel digital post-distortion (DPoD) technique designed to improve the received signal quality in multi-user orthogonal frequency-division multiple access (OFDMA) systems under severe power amplifier (PA) nonlinearities in the involved transmitters. The proposed approach combines memory polynomial-based nonlinear distortion modeling and cancellation with practical parameter estimation using demodulation reference signals (DMRS). Importantly, a new extended DMRS structure and corresponding receiver signal processing solutions are proposed which enable the receiver to mitigate both the ordinary pass-band nonlinearities as well as nonlinear crosstalk or interference between multiple frequency-multiplexed transmitters operating simultaneously within the channel bandwidth. Extensive numerical evaluations conducted in a 5G NR uplink scenario at 28GHz demonstrate significant receiver performance improvements, measured through effective error vector magnitudes (EVMs) for the frequency-multiplexed waveforms. The results validate the method’s effectiveness even under aggressive PA nonlinearities and high-order modulation schemes including 64-QAM and 256-QAM, thus paving the way towards improved power-efficiency and coverage in future OFDMA networks.

title: Data Quality in Machine Learning: Boosting the Base for Solid AI Systems
authors: Vidya Sagar S D, Lohith M S, Dileep M R
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Data quality is fundamental to the success of machine learning models. As these models increasingly drive critical decisions across various sectors, ensuring that the underlying data is accurate, consistent, and complete becomes paramount. This paper examines the multiple dimensions of data quality and their impact on machine learning outcomes. We analyse current research and practical scenarios to address common challenges such as noise, missing data, and imbalanced datasets, proposing a systematic approach to assess and enhance data quality. We demonstrate that effective data quality management not only improves model accuracy but also produces more reliable and interpretable results. Through comprehensive data profiling, preprocessing techniques, and continuous monitoring, our approach shows measurable improvements in precision, efficiency, and bias reduction. We provide an extensive literature review with over 20 relevant references and present schematic diagrams that highlight the strategic importance of data quality as a cornerstone of modern AI systems.

title: Fairness-Aware Offline Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (Fair-RLHF): Mitigating Bias in Human Preferences
authors: Sheryl Mathew, N Harshit
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Offline Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback aims to train AI agents using predefined human data, however human feedback can be biased which can lead to unfair or sub-optimal decision-making. In this paper, we propose Fairness-Aware Offline RLHF (Fair-RLHF), a framework designed to integrate fairness constraints into Offline Reinforcement Learning. We aim to detect and mitigate bias in Human Feedback. This is done using causal graphs (DAGs), which help in analyzing and identifying where bias originates. This visual representation of the cause-effect relationship determines unfair dependencies and uses fairness techniques to remove them. Furthermore, fairness constraints are applied that use Pareto efficiency to balance fairness with performance, achieving the precision of 46.5% and 84%. It is optimized by ensuring fairness and maximizing performance. This work provides a fair and efficient RLHF model that aims to bridge the gap between reinforcement learning, fairness constraints, and human feedback by developing a framework that ensures AI agents are unbiased and demographic disparities are reduced.

title: OPTISCHED: A Cognitive Algorithm for Streamlined IoT Task Scheduling in Cloud Computing
authors: Sangeetha K, Kanthimathi M, Anuratha K, Harini T, Hamrithaa Shree A, Kaviya M
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Cloud computing is a most used technology that gives access to the user and manage resources such as storage, data bases and applications over the internet. There are several cloud services for wide variety of uses, so efficient task scheduling in cloud computing is important as it ensures optimised consumption. So, we propose a solution for optimised power consumption by reducing overall makespan. We have taken up overall makespan as well as power consumption as our proposed algorithm’s [Optisched] performance metrics. This paper works on the comparison of proposed scheduling algorithm, i.e Optisched with other existing algorithms to overcome the drawbacks of these scheduling algorithm by certain percentage. When the Virtual machines are increased while tasks being assigned through Optisched, the overall makespan is reduced. Due to the increase in virtual machine along with our optisched algorithm, overall execution time is reduced by completely avoiding idle time in the virtual machines through streamlined task scheduling as of which energy wastage is also eliminated. Through this, we can achieve a streamlined usage of IoT Task scheduling.

title: FPGA Stereo Visual Slam with Efficient Stereo Feature Matching and Key-Frame Generation
authors: Miyuru Thathsara, Damith Anhettigama, Siew-Kei Lam
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: Stereo visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) enables autonomous agents to navigate complex environments by simultaneously building metric maps and localizing within them. In this paper, we present the first FPGA-based System-on-Chip (SoC) implementation of stereo visual SLAM. Our design reduces stereo feature matching latency by dynamically grouping descriptors based on localized image regions and descriptor similarity, while preserving matching accuracy. We also revisit conventional key-frame generation strategies and show that some criteria lead to unnecessary map expansion and runtime instability on resource-constrained platforms. To address this, we propose a new key-frame generation method based on the tracking status of local map points in the current frame. Extensive evaluations on standard benchmarks show that our system achieves high robustness and real-time performance with notable performance-power gains, while maintaining accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art stereo SLAM methods on embedded platforms. Real-world experiments further confirm our system's suitability for practical robotic applications.

title: Supervised Learning Approach to Infrared Image Based Condition Assessment of 33kV Lightning Surge Arresters
authors: Raveesha Illesinghe, Manjula Fernando, Nisal Paranawithana
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: This paper presents an online condition assessment method for 33kV Silicon rubber (SIR) housed ZnO surge arresters through infrared imaging. A total of 46 arrester samples including field-energized arresters, lab-tested arresters (field aged and a virgin arrester) were analyzed by infrared images and grouped into three "health" classes by laboratory measurements and modelling. Insulation resistance (IR) and leakage current (LC) measurements were taken for lab-tested arresters, whereas Finite Element Method (FEM) based modeling was used to assess field-energized samples. An infrared image consisted of 180 features and by Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), 180-dimensional thermal data were reduced to a 2D space with the three classification labels. Decision boundaries for classifying arresters were defined using Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifiers with their accuracy evaluated for effective arrester condition assessment.

title: Integrated Feature Extraction Using Principal Components and Deep Autoencoder With Extra Trees for Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Kumari K, Uma Mageswari, Khushbu Pandey, A. Pushpalatha, Girish Kalele, Balamurugan V
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The study suggests a modern Intrusion Detection System (IDS) that uses PCA (Principal Component Analysis) and an UDAE (Unsupervised Deep Autoencoder) for good feature extraction, then uses the Extra Tree (ET) classification method. It tackles the typical issues of working with many attributes and an uneven distribution of labels in network flow datasets. At the start, data is balanced and SMOTE-ENN is used to help minority classes be better represented. PCA separates data with strong linear relationships and only keeps strong and moderate main components in the analysis. These elements go through UDAE which looks for non-linear patterns and makes sure the output feature set is as reduced as possible. All the features are then labeled by using machine learning models and ET shows the highest accuracy. The proposed PC-UDAE framework was tested against popular benchmark sets and showed better accuracy, F1-score and faster speed than previous methods. Research demonstrates that the model outperforms other approaches which makes it a solid and dependable solution for detecting many kinds of cyberattacks.

title: Higher-order Monte Carlo cluster dynamics for community detection in Euclidean graphs
authors: Louis Hauseux, Nahuel Soprano-Loto, Konstantin Avrachenkov
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We study Gibbs distributions with competing interactions and propose a higher-order extension of the Swendsen-Wang dynamics that incorporates triangular bonds. The new dynamics preserves the same stationary distribution, alleviates frustration, and yields markedly better sampling. When applied to a synthetic Euclidean-graph community-detection benchmark, our algorithm outperforms existing methods.

title: High Level Synthesis Design for Soft-Input GRAND
authors: Tuomas Aaltonen, Mikko Valkama, Syed Mohsin Abbas
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Guessing Random Additive Noise Decoding (GRAND) is a universal channel code decoder for short linear block codes. GRAND can be implemented efficiently on hardware and features both hard-input and soft-input variants. Hardware implementations based on GRAND range from those that employ sequential error pattern generation to reduce resource consumption at the expense of higher decoding latency to those that employ parallel error pattern generation to reduce decoding latency at the expense of higher resource utilization. In this work, we present a soft-input Ordered Reliability Bits GRAND (ORBGRAND) hardware implementation framework based on High-Level Synthesis (HLS). The proposed HLS-based methodology simplifies Design Space Exploration (DSE) for ORBGRAND hardware implementations targeted at applications with varying constraints on decoding latency and resource utilization. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first-of-its-kind HLS-based soft-input ORBGRAND hardware implementation framework, and the source code for the proposed framework is provided under a permissive open-source license.

title: An Adaptive Tuning Method for Neutron Spectrum Unfolding Based on Supervised-Autoencoder with Few-Channel Detection
authors: Chenglong Cao, Pei Cao, Qingbo Zhou, Ming Sun
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: The neutron spectrum plays a critical role in ensuring reactor operational safety. However, online neutron spectrum measurement methods still face challenges in achieving high accuracy. Although multilayer perceptron (MLP)-based artificial neural networks (ANNs) have been applied in spectrum unfolding, their performance is often limited by insufficient training data and convergence difficulties. To address these issues, this study developed an adaptive tuning method for neutron spectrum unfolding based on Supervised-Autoencoder with few-channel detection. A large dataset of diverse neutron spectra from fast reactors was simulated by a Monte Carlo program. Simulated detector counts were generated using pre-calibrated response functions. An autoencoder was employed to encode and decode spectral data, enabling the trained decoder to rapidly and accurately predict neutron spectra directly from detector counts. The method was validated using a spectral library comprising neutron spectra from the CLEAR-I, RBEC-M, and BN-600 fast reactor cores. Results demonstrated that, compared to conventional MLP-based approaches, the proposed method reduced the mean squared error (MSE) by 67.34%, the average relative deviation (ARD) by 50.37%, and the spectrum quality (Qs) by 42.55%, confirming its improved reliability in neutron spectrum unfolding.

title: Detecting Online Hate Using Emotion, Sarcasm, and Visual Cues: A Multimodal Deep Learning Approach
authors: Pratha Shetty, Ravindra B V, Nisha, Adarsh, Ethan Hadley Rodrigues
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Hate speech is racing across social networks, choking the online space with danger, as bitter smoke fills a packed room. Traditional text models often overlook subtle hate, especially when it sneaks in as a sarcastic jab, a clipped, biting tone, or a meme whose grin cuts deeper than the caption. This study introduces a multimodal deep learning framework that brings together text, emotion, sarcasm, and visual signals, such as a quick smile or a sharp tone to boost detection accuracy. The model blends DeBERTa (Decoding-enhanced BERT with Disentangled Attention)'s nuanced understanding of text with DistilBERT (Distilled Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)'s ability to capture emotional and sarcastic cues, and CNN (Convolutional Neural Network)'s sharp eye for visual details enhanced by OCR (Optical Character Recognition) for embedded text, weaving them together to spot hateful content with precision. The tests revealed that the method beats single-modality baselines, catching hateful language with sharper accuracy and a better grasp of context, such as detecting the sting in an offhand remark.

title: SmartSphere: Bridging AI-Powered Automation and Tesla Coil Wireless Energy for Smarter Living
authors: Jerun Kingston Jurison Jenaraj, Ayushyaa Umasuthan, Vithiyasahar Vigneswaran, Kithusshand Raveendran, Kanagasabai Thiruthanigesan, Sanvitha Kasthuriarachchi
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: This paper presents SmartSphere, an intelligent, secure, and wire-free home automation system that synergizes artificial intelligence, multi-factor access control, and resonant wireless power transmission. Built on an edge-computing architecture using ESP32 and Arduino Mega 2560 microcontrollers, SmartSphere integrates facial recognition, fingerprint authentication (96.7% accuracy), and IR-based presence detection to reduce false activations by 75%. The system employs OpenCV and TensorFlow Lite for real-time anomaly detection and environmental personalization, including weather-responsive adjustments and emotion-aware lighting via facial expression analysis. A key innovation is the incorporation of a Tesla coil-based wireless power transmission module, which eliminates conventional wiring constraints and reduces installation cabling by 86%. Experimental validation demonstrates a 14% improvement in energy efficiency, 75% faster response time (0.3 s), and seamless compatibility with 92% of tested IoT devices. Through comprehensive testing and validation, this research establishes SmartSphere as a secure, intelligent, and sustainable solution for next-generation smart homes, addressing the limitations of traditional wired systems while enhancing the user experience through AI-driven personalization.

title: An Innovative Wavelet Neural Network Framework for Glaucoma Detection using Statistical and Graph-Based Feature Analysis of Retinal Images
authors: Monika Khatkar, Naman Pratap Singh Nain, Sandeep Mathur, Nyangam Rime, Jagdeesh Ahirwar, B. Sireesha
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: A complete automated system for diagnosing glaucoma is introduced in this study using a Wavelet Neural Network (WNN). First, the method applies a strong retinal image pre-processing to improve strength and clarity using Quantile-based histogram altering and using a Quadratic Rank Transmutation Map on the image's cumulative distribution function. A two-stage image enhancement process is applied next, using multi-scale morphological operations, dynamically produced structuring elements and Dual-Tree Complex Wavelet Transform (DT-CWT) coefficients. After improving the retinal images, a scheme combining different types of features is used. Analysis is performed by applying biorthogonal wavelets to measure first-order, second-order, higher-order cumulant (HoC) and higher-order spectral (HoS) metrics. At the same time, graph features are generated using Local Graph Structures (LGS) and Graph Shortest Paths (GSP) in various directions, so that both nearby and wide-ranging textures of retinal pictures are retained. A combination of statistics and structural characteristics produces the final feature vector which means learning patterns is more reliable. WNN, with Mexican hat wavelet-based activation functions, is put to work for classification as a method. Tests on ORIGA, ACRIMA and DRISHTI datasets prove that our WNN model shows higher accuracy and lower error than MLP, confirming its usefulness for identifying early glaucoma. Overall, the suggested technique gets a 96.36% success rate on the ORIGA dataset. This points to a very dependable glaucoma categorization system.

title: Parameters Identification of MESFET by Including Thermal Effect, Surface and Substrate Trappings Using Modified Hippopotamus Optimization
authors: S. Thulasi Prasad, M Madhusudhan Reddy, Satti Sudha Mohan Reddy, Shuvanka Maji, Smruti Ranjan Nayak, G.Simi Margarat, M Naresh Kumar, Ajay Sudhir Bale, Kandi Bhanu Prakash
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Metal Semiconductor Field Effect Transistors (MESFETs) configured in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 H}$</tex>-SiC are utilized in various applications that impact our daily lives. The analysis of MESFET models under direct current characteristics is quite complex. This paper considers three main effects: surface trapping, thermal effects, and substrate trapping, to elucidate the direct current characteristics of the MESFET. The suggested model for the current-voltage (I-V) curves incorporates the Caughey-Thomas configuration. This configuration takes into account the trapping of electrons in the substrate through various deep-level trapping methods. Additionally, it addresses the field-dependent mobility of electrons and provides an analysis of the two-dimensional distribution of charge located beneath the gate. This comprehensive approach allows for a better understanding of the electronic behavior in the device under different operating conditions. This proposed method is comprehensive, accounting for all significant thermal effects and material flaws, including traps. Nonetheless, solving intricate mathematical equations of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 H}$</tex>-SiC MESFET to derive precise I-V characteristics across various regions is quite challenging due to its complexity. Consequently, the Hippopotamus Optimization Method (HOM) is employed to address a range of intricate equations related to 4H-SiC MESFET, thereby accurately deriving the I-V characteristics. Consequently, the performance of the suggested analytical model closely resembles that of a real-time <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 H - S i C}$</tex> MESFET. Various behavioral characteristics are obtained and evaluated using the MATLAB programming software.

title: Deep Learning Approach for Parkinson's Disease Detection Using Voice Feature
authors: Esha, Nagesh U B, Manojrao, Oliva Mary Fernandes, Srajesh Shetty
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Parkinson's disease moves in quietly, progressing over time, so catching it early without invasive tests is crucial. This study presents a detailed framework that uses subtle changes in voice like a faint pitch tremor or a slight shimmer to help identify Parkinson's disease. We compare seven models ranging from classic machine learning classifiers to a 1D Convolutional Neural Network using the standardized Parkinson's Voice Dataset, where each voice clip carries the faint tremor of the condition. Our results show that ensemble methods work best for diagnosing voices, and the XGBoost classifier topped the charts with a 94.87 percentage accuracy rate. Alongside the voice module, the system packs a second unit built to analyze spiral and wave images, tracing their curves as if following ripples in water. This module uses a pre-trained CNN and applies Grad-CAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) to make its predictions easier to understand, lighting up the exact parts of a drawing like a dark corner or a curved line that steer its decision. The study confirms that voice features can predict outcomes with striking accuracy, sets a high bar for performance, and shows that an integrated, multimodal, explainable AI system could realistically guide future clinical decisions much like a clear, confident voice cutting through background noise.

title: Deep Convolutional Neural Network-Based Human Bone Classification with Multi-View Medical Image Transformation
authors: Markala Karthik, N Prabhakaran, Trapty Agarwal, R. Nallakumar, Nimesh Raj, Mary Gladence L
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Medical imaging of human bones classification is an important part of the diagnosis and treatment plan. In this study, a DCNN (Deep Convolutional Neural Network) method of automatically classifying human bones with the use of multiview medical image transformations is presented. With the help of Musculoskeletal Radiographs (MURA) dataset containing 40,005 X-ray images of seven different categories, the proposed model is trained on 36,808 images and tested on 3,197 images. The architecture consists of four convolutional layers, four pooling layers, one flattened layer and two dense layers with a dropout rate of 0.2 to avoid overfitting. The experimental findings also indicate that the DCNN converges at an accuracy of 96.37 percent and precision of 95.45 percent, implying its strength and predictive performance. The method shows a great promise in aiding radiologists in the classification of bones in the correct manner, improving the speed and accuracy of the clinical workflow.

title: Feedback-Assisted Decentralized Q-Learning for Dynamic Spectrum Access
authors: Ghilas Aissou, Salman Habib, Rémi A. Chou, Taejoon Kim
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We present an approach to Decentralized Dynamic Spectrum Access (DDSA) in temporally correlated frequency channels. The problem is formulated as a Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP) to capture the inherent partial observability and non-stationarity in DDSA. We first propose a novel state representation technique that captures the observation history, providing the temporal context needed to mitigate partial observability. This representation scales linearly with the length of the history window, avoiding the exponential state space growth of traditional history-based methods. The state representation evaluates a score function over the history to achieve a controllable aliasing error, with increasing computational overhead with window size. Building on this representation, we propose a decentralized Q-learning algorithm with a two-phase reward structure driven by individual 1-bit feedback sent by the receiver and a global 1-bit feedback sent by a moderator. The non-stationarity in Dec-POMDPs is addressed by a policy update mechanism that leverages the 1-bit global feedback to align agent behaviors and stabilize learning. The proposed algorithm is then validated through simulation.

title: Omnidirectional Recognition of Medical Emergency Situations Through Body Gesture Using 360-Degree Camera
authors: Cherry Joy L. Escoña, Anne Jeline R. Lazaga, Ma. Aubrey D. Mensote, Lea Grace C. Obtinalla, Sherwin Vasquez, Engr. Roselito E. Tolentino
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: This paper presents an omnidirectional system for recognizing medical emergency situations through body gestures. This research addresses the significant limitations of previous systems which often relies on traditional cameras with a restricted field-of-view. A 360-degree camera has been utilized by the system for omnidirectional gesture recognition of body gestures. The researchers constructed MediaPipe for real-time pose estimation and a Random Forest classifier trained on five emergency gestures: headache, cough, chest pain, vomiting, and fall. The system achieved a gesture recognition reliability of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5. 3 7 \%}$</tex> across multiple angles and distances, and a combined recognition-response success rate of 96.2 % in robot-assisted medicine dispensing and emergency alert triggering. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of omnidirectional vision in enabling robust, angle-independent gesture recognition suitable for medical monitoring applications.

title: Designing Emotionally Intelligent Embodied Agents for Immersive Virtual Reality Experiences
authors: Seyedbehnam Rad, Razeen Hussain, Manuela Chessa, Fabio Solari
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: The integration of conversational agents in virtual reality (VR) offers promising opportunities for applications such as training, education, and mental health support. However, most current VR agents lack empathic capabilities, which are crucial for fostering engagement, trust, and effective interaction. To address this gap, we present EMBRACE, a modular framework that combines natural language processing, emotion inference, expressive text-to-speech synthesis, and AI-driven facial animation to deliver emotionally responsive VR agents. We implemented EMBRACE within a counselling-inspired virtual environment and conducted a user study, comparing empathic and nonempathic agent conditions. Subjective measures included usability, presence, and agent evaluation, while objective measures incorporated heart rate and heart rate variability. Results show that empathic behaviours enhanced spatial presence and revealed trends toward higher general presence and physiological engagement. Additional analyses indicated that prior chatbot experience shaped usability and agent ratings, while gender influenced certain presence dimensions. Our findings demonstrate both the feasibility and benefits of implementing empathic VR agents.

title: Resource-Aware Multiobjective Neural Architecture Search for Low-Light Image Enhancement on Mobile Devices
authors: Bin Cao, Haixuan Feng
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: In the nighttime low-light setting, there is not enough light and the contrast is low. This greatly lowers the accuracy of detecting pedestrians and vehicles in mobile intelligent transportation systems. As a result, it raises the chance of traffic accidents. To address this problem, this paper presents the resource-aware multiobjective neural architecture search for low-light image enhancement (RAMONAS-LIE). This method constructs a lightweight deep curve estimation network in a dynamic way. It improves the visual quality of low-light images by adjusting brightness at the pixel level. In addition, to improve search efficiency and variety in architectures, it introduces a dualcriteria elite selection evolutionary algorithm (NSGAIII-DCES). Experimental results show that RAMONAS-LIE provides the better image enhancement, demonstrating its practicality for mobile applications.

title: LLM-Assisted Ontology-Based Task-Oriented Data Exchange Paradigm: A Case Study
authors: Shiyao Zhang, Xue Feng, Yuji Dong, Jie Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Smart cities depend on cross-domain data exchange, yet its effectiveness is limited by semantic heterogeneity and privacy-induced information asymmetry, which complicate traditional integration. Although ontology alignment can resolve semantic disparities, it struggles under incomplete knowledge conditions. Leveraging the emergent capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), this paper introduces an LLM-centric, task-oriented paradigm that automates ontology alignment and enables data exchange under realistic constraints. A case study demonstrates the approach's practicality in facilitating semantic interoperability without full information disclosure, highlighting its potential for privacy-aware smart city applications.

title: Deep Semantic Matching Based on Selective Feature Attention and Divide-and-Conquer Strategy
authors: Yan Ren, Chaocheng Zhang, Baokang Li
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Traditional semantic matching models in question answering (QA) and dialogue tasks often process all tokens uniformly, overlooking the varying granularity required for matching key components in user queries — such as actions, entities, and events. This leads to insufficient recognition of critical semantic information and abstract intents, thereby compromising matching accuracy and user experience. To address this issue, this paper proposes a deep semantic matching method based on selective feature attention and a divide-and-conquer strategy. Specifically, the model employs a keyword-intent separation mechanism to perform fine-grained matching for keywords and coarsegrained matching for intents. Additionally, a selective feature attention mechanism dynamically adjusts the importance of semantic features, enhancing the model's ability to capture essential semantics. Without incurring additional inference costs, the proposed method achieves robust and effective performance improvements across multiple benchmark datasets. This is because our keyword - intent separation and selective attention modules are applied only during training, and the inference stage uses exactly the same encoder and classifier architecture as the baseline model. The results demonstrate that the approach provides strong support for QA matching tasks.

title: InSAR-Based Monitoring of Ground Deformation at Grasberg Open-Pit Mine, Indonesia (2017-2025)
authors: Arif Aditiya, Takeo Ito, Agustan Agustan, Moh Fifik Syafiudin, Djoko Nugroho, Ali Nurmoko, Muhammad Iqbal Habibie
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Monitoring ground deformation in large-scale mining areas is critical for operational safety and geohazard mitigation. The Grasberg Mine in Indonesia, one of the world’s largest open-pit mining complexes, presents unique challenges due to its steep topography, tropical climate, and intensive excavation. In this study, surface displacement from 2017 to July 2025 was analyzed using ascending and descending C-band Sentinel-1 InSAR data, corrected for atmospheric delays with ERA5 reanalysis. Results show cumulative subsidence at selected points around -20 cm in the ascending track, while the descending track reveals uplift of approximately 17 cm. Decomposition of the displacement field highlights opposing east-west motions on opposite pit flanks, indicating mass movement and stress distribution converging into the Pit and confirming typical contraction patterns in the Open-Pit mining environments. Temporal change detection using the Pruned Exact Linear Time algorithm identified 12 distinct deformation phases, reflecting the episodic nature of mining-induced ground motion. These findings emphasize the importance of multigeometry InSAR combined with change detection for mitigating slope instability, guiding risk management strategies, and integrating geodetic and geotechnical models into mining safety frameworks.

title: Maximum FPGA: A 32K-Point 32-Parallel Floating Point FFT
authors: Martin Langhammer, Bogdan Pasca
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Field-Programmable Logic and Applications (FPL)
abstract: FPGAs offer a powerful and flexible platform to build complex systems on. But the potential - both in density and clock frequency - is often missed. In this work we present a massively parallel FFT, which can process a 32 K point FFT with 32-parallel IEEE-754 single-precision floatingpoint streams of complex data. Each core uses 1281 DSP Blocks, and can be packed into a near 100 % DSP Block density in an Altera Agilex-7 FPGA. Six such cores have been instantiated in a system, using 90 % of the device capability. In all cases our designs close timing at nearly 770 MHz, which is the restricted frequency of the DSP Block in floatingpoint mode. The contributions of this work are manifold. We demonstrate approaches that can be used to fill even larger FPGAs to high density with high performance. We also introduce optimizations for the FFT that can be used by all types of (including non-FFT) applications, from simple implementations to the very large parallel examples we use to demonstrate our research.

title: Privacy-Preserving Heart Disease Prediction Using TabTransformer-Based Federated Learning on Non-IID Clinical Data
authors: Chamudika Pallewela
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: Heart disease remains a leading cause of mortality worldwide, and accurate early prediction is critical for timely clinical intervention. Traditional machine learning and deep learning approaches often rely on centralised patient data, raising significant concerns regarding privacy, security, and regulatory compliance. In this study, the researcher proposes a privacy-preserving federated learning framework that integrates a TabTransformer architecture with the FedProx optimisation algorithm to predict heart disease from tabular clinical data. The framework is specifically designed to address non-identically distributed (non-IID) client data across multiple healthcare institutions, ensuring stable model convergence while preserving data confidentiality. Comprehensive evaluations including accuracy, F1-score, precision, recall, ROC-AUC, and loss—demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms conventional federated models and achieves robust performance at both client and global levels. These findings underscore the potential of transformer-based federated learning as a scalable, privacy-preserving solution for heart disease prediction, paving the way for real-world deployment in clinical environments.In addition to demonstrating strong predictive accuracy, the proposed framework is benchmarked against baseline FL and centralised models, showing improved generalisation under heterogeneous conditions. A privacy-preserving protocol and interpretability analysis further highlight its practicality for real-world deployments.

title: Adaptive Fuzzy-Neural AI Models for Dynamic Environment Control
authors: Vamsi Krishna Velpuri, Mayank Atreya, Prashanthi Pala, Ponnarasan Krishnan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Adaptive control strategies are required in dynamic systems, like smart buildings, autonomous vehicles, and industrial IoT systems that need to be able to deal with uncertainty, nonlinearity, and fast contextual variation. In these conditions, the traditional methods that are rule-based or neural approaches in general do not tend to be robust and interpretable. The paper suggests Adaptive Fuzzy-Neural AI Models which integrate the power of reasoning of fuzzy logic with that of neural networks to adaptively and in real-time make decisions. The suggested models dynamically adapt fuzzy membership functions using neural adaptation and could finetune them in highly volatile and unstructured environments. It proposes a hybrid framework that combines fuzzy reasoning and deep reinforcement learning to achieve context-dependent adaptation. The experimental tests which are done on benchmark dynamic control situations such as climate regulation, swarm robotics navigation and smart grid load balancing, have shown faster convergence speeds and stability and accuracy over traditional fuzzy systems or neural systems. The findings indicate that the adaptive fuzzy-neural structure can decrease the error rates to a maximum of 18, increase resilience to noisy inputs and can effectively scale to more complex systems. The results define the possibility of the fuzzyneural AI as a powerful paradigm of intelligent control in dynamic, safety-critical, and resource-constrained settings.

title: Wireless Link Scheduling with State-Augmented Graph Neural Networks
authors: Romina Garcia Camargo, Zhiyang Wang, Navid NaderiAlizadeh, Alejandro Ribeiro
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We study a long-term formulation of link scheduling in large-scale wireless networks. Existing approaches maximize the instantaneous sum rate; however, while wireless networks exhibit highly dynamic behavior, end users generally experience performance in expectation. This motivates the optimization of time-averaged performance metrics. We formulate link scheduling as a constrained optimization problem in which the objective is to maximize the sum rate averaged over a series of time slots. Furthermore, we impose a minimum transmission requirement that guarantees each link is scheduled for at least a fixed fraction of the total time. We represent the network by modeling users and links as nodes and edges and construct the corresponding conflict graph to capture interference. We operate in the Lagrangian dual domain and parameterize the scheduling policy with a Graph Neural Network (GNN). To address the challenge of long-term performance optimization, we adopt a state-augmentation technique: by incorporating the Lagrangian dual variables as dynamic inputs to the GNN, the policy can gradually adapt its scheduling decisions to satisfy the time-average constraints. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed policy through numerical simulations.

title: Ziv-Zakai Bound for Near-Field Localization and Sensing
authors: Nicolò Decarli, Davide Dardari
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: The increasing carrier frequencies and growing physical dimensions of antenna arrays in modern wireless systems are driving renewed interest in localization and sensing under near-field conditions. In this paper, we analyze the Ziv-Zakai Bound (ZZB) for near-field localization and sensing operated with large antenna arrays, which offers a tighter characterization of estimation accuracy compared to traditional bounds such as the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB), especially in low signal-to-noise ratio or threshold regions. Leveraging spherical wavefront and array geometry in the signal model, we evaluate the ZZB for distance and angle estimation, investigating the dependence of the accuracy on key signal and system parameters such as array geometry, wavelength, and target position. Our analysis highlights the transition behavior of the ZZB and underscores the fundamental limitations and opportunities for accurate near-field sensing.

title: Land Suitability Mapping for Agrivoltaic System in Agusan Del Sur Using Geographic Information System (GIS) and Analytic Hierarchy Process
authors: Julia Marie A. Condolon, Richelle Mae U. Intic, Maria Belinda D. Campana, Arnaldo C. Gagula
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Energy transition is crucial for long-term growth and climate resilience, offering sustainable development solutions. As global energy demand increases due to population growth and industrial intensification, addressing climate change requires shifting from fossil fuels to renewables. However, largescale solar installations require extensive land allocation, posing challenges for agricultural production. The concept of Agrivoltaics, combining solar energy production and agriculture on the same land, has emerged to address this issue. This study focuses on identifying suitable areas for implementing Agrivoltaic systems in Agusan del Sur, a province in the Philippines. Geographic Information System (GIS) techniques and the Analytical Hierarchy Process (AHP) approach are utilized to assess land suitability based on various criteria, including solar radiation, slope, land cover, aspect, elevation, soil type, and distance to roads and protected areas, which serve as exclusion factors. Results show that the suitability of agrivoltaic installations is highly dependent on Solar Radiation availability, followed by slope of the area, Land Cover, Aspect, Elevation, Soil Type, and Proximity to Roads. The results also provide a weighted overlay suitability map, highlighting the most suitable areas for Agrivoltaic Systems installation, which are the municipalities of Prosperidad, San Francisco, and Rosario in the province of Agusan del Sur.

title: Adaptive Store-and-Recall Memory for Domain Continual Learning
authors: Shutian Zhang, Changsheng Li, Guoren Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Domain continual learning requires overcoming catastrophic forgetting while training in sequential domains. A common solution is to store and replay samples from previous domains to preserve knowledge, but such replay-based strategies raise privacy and storage concerns and are often impractical in real-world settings. In this paper, we propose a rehearsal-free framework, which leverages a store-and-recall strategy to generate a compatible optimization subspace, ensuring effective transfer for preventing catastrophic forgetting. To establish a foundation for effectively leveraging historical knowledge, we introduce a Frequency-Aware Filtering, which extracts key representations in the frequency domain and compacts them into internalmemory to support rehearsal-free retention. To further exploit historical information effectively, we design a Gain-Adaptive Gating mechanism integrated with a Knowledge Fusion module, which selectively activates historical knowledge based on gain signals to avoid negative transfer caused by distribution shifts. Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art continual learning methods across standard benchmarks under the domain-incremental setting.

title: Extremum-Based Joint Compression and Detection for Distributed Sensing
authors: Amir Weiss, Alejandro Lancho
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: We study joint compression and detection in distributed sensing systems motivated by emerging applications such as IoT-based localization. Two spatially separated sensors observe noisy signals and can exchange only a k-bit message over a reliable one-way low-rate link. One sensor compresses its observation into a k-bit description to help the other decide whether their observations share a common underlying signal or are statistically independent. We propose a simple extremum-based strategy, in which the encoder sends the index of its largest sample and the decoder performs a scalar threshold test. We derive exact nonasymptotic false-alarm and misdetection probabilities and validate the analysis with representative simulations.

title: Explainable AI in Maze Classification and Segmentation: A Systematic Review
authors: Enugula HariKrishna, Durgesh Nandan
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) has recently been introduced as a paradigm for ensuring transparency, interpretability and increasing trust in complex ML systems. In a segmental or classification context in the maze area, XAI allows for an insight on model behavior, supporting the validation of the algorithm and the human understanding of the internal decision process. In this systematic review, we summarize the existing work on explainable approaches to computational tasks related to mazes, considering approaches based on feature attribution, rule-based explanation, visualization, and model simplification. Moreover, the review explains how models can be re-trained with XAI techniques, how XAI can be adapted to segmentation problems and how interpretability can improve robustness and generalization. Furthermore, we classify studies according to methodology, datasets, and evaluation metrics and determine main trends, strengths, and weaknesses across the literature. Finally, a discussion highlights research gaps and directions for future research that can improve the reliability and interpretability of machine learning-based maze classification and segmentation.

title: Intelligent Watering System for Crop Fields Using IoT-Based Automation and Environmental Sensing
authors: Mohammad Maroof Siddiqui
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: Optimal use of water is the most critical factor in agricultural productivity, particularly in water-short and erratic-rainfall areas. Conventional irrigation frequently leads to water waste and low crop yield, translate into high operational costs. This paper presents the design and deployment of IWS (Intelligent Watering System) for crop fields using Internet of Things (IoT) technologies and Environmental Sensors. It can sense soil moisture, temperature and humidity and adds intelligence to irrigation process using real-time data analytics. In this system intended to make better use of water and get the highest crop yield out of it, with a goal toward reducing human input as well as time and money. In addition, wireless communication modules are incorporated for remote monitoring and control, and the decision-making algorithms can realize the adaptive response of field status based on changes. The proposed solution is scalable and inexpensive, which can be employed for both small and large farms. Using IoT based platforms and sensor networks, the proposed system has a potential applicability for sustainable farming practices, environmental stress reduction as well as precision agriculture in water stressed areas.

authors: N/A

title: How to and Who Should Read This Book
authors: N/A

title: Attitude Estimated Constraint Circular Motion Control for Underactuated Unicycle Robots_supp1-3677722.mp4
authors: Pushkal Purohit
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of motion confinement for an underactuated unicycle robot while stabilizing it along a desired circular trajectory. It is considered that only the robot's position is available for feedback, and its heading angle measurements are not available. To address this, we design an observer for the robot's position and heading angle estimates using only available position measurements. Leveraging these state estimates and the barrier Lyapunov function (BLF), we propose a variable gain-based control design relying on a composite Lyapunov function that simultaneously captures the desired control objectives and convergence of observer errors. It was shown that the proposed controller not only asymptotically stabilizes the motion of the unicycle robot on the desired circular path but also restricts its trajectories within the predefined circular boundary almost everywhere. Furthermore, we elaborate on how the state constraints imposed in the original problem formulation are effectively enforced through the estimated variables. Both numerical simulations and real-time experiments on the Khepera IV robot are provided.

title: On the Importance of Tactile Sensing for Imitation Learning: A Case Study on Robotic Match Lighting_supp2-3677716.pdf
authors: Niklas Funk
abstract: The field of robotic manipulation has advanced significantly in recent years. At the sensing level, several novel tactile sensors have been developed, capable of providing accurate contact information. On a methodological level, learning from demonstrations has proven an efficient paradigm to obtain performant robotic manipulation policies. The combination of both holds the promise to extract crucial contact-related information from the demonstration data and actively exploit it during policy rollouts. However, this integration has so far been underexplored, most notably in dynamic, contact-rich manipulation tasks where precision and reactivity are essential. This work therefore proposes a multimodal, visuotactile imitation learning framework that integrates a modular transformer architecture with a flow-based generative model, enabling efficient learning of fast and dexterous manipulation policies. We evaluate our framework on the dynamic, contact-rich task of robotic match lighting - a task in which tactile feedback influences human manipulation performance. The experimental results highlight the effectiveness of our approach and show that adding tactile information improves policy performance, thereby underlining their combined potential for learning dynamic manipulation from few demonstrations.

title: Automated Detection of Middle Ear Infections Using Squeeze-and-Excitation ConvNeXt
authors: M. Rethina Kumari, V. Anusuya
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Children and adults are prone to ear infections, especially otitis media, which cause serious problems if left untreated. Traditional diagnostic approaches rely mainly on visual examinations, which is subjective and variable amongst practitioners. A computer-aided detection system that uses an effective image processing and Deep Learning (DL) architecture to automate the identification of ear infections has been proposed as a solution to this problem. Hence, this works deploys an enhanced Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) - ConvNeXt model proposed for automated detection of middle ear infections. The proposed approach begins with Bilateral Filter in preprocessing stage to eliminate noise while keeping edge structures that are critical for accurate diagnosis. In order to improve the separation of healthy and sick areas in otoscopic images, cluster pixel regions are then subjected to Gaussian Kernel Mean Shift Segmentation. Boundary Descriptors are retrieved to emphasize the infection zones' form and edge-based characteristics for effective depiction. It provides compact and discriminative shape-based characteristics, are then used to capture the boundary features of segmented regions. These characteristics are then classified using enhanced SE ConvNeXt model, which combines the capabilities of ConvNeXt architecture with SE attention mechanism to improve feature selectivity and diagnostic accuracy. Experimental assessments using Python software show that this framework achieves good accuracy of 99.97%, precision of 96.9%, and recall of 96.9% in detecting middle ear infections. With its ability to diagnose earrelated problems more quickly, accurately, and non-invasively, the proposed approach shows possibilities for inclusion into clinical decision support systems.

title: Quantum Machine Learning for Disease Prediction: A Comparative and Multimodal Study on Early-Stage and Chronic Conditions
authors: Nitika Bahl, Pranjal Singh, Kulvinder Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) is fast becoming a powerful instrument in medical diagnostics because it can process high-dimensional data and small samples efficiently. This paper is a detailed analysis of QML models used for disease prediction, with a relative comparison of Variational Quantum Classifiers (VQC), Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN), and Quantum Support Vector Machines (QSVM) in clinical environments. We suggest a new multimodal quantumclassical hybrid approach that analyzes electronic health records (EHR), medical imaging, and biosignal data by means of dedicated QML pipelines with late fusion for ultimate decisionmaking. Recent IEEE literature is integrated in experimental performance glimpses to demonstrate the merits, difficulties, and viability of QML in real-world healthcare applications. The envisioned architecture provides the foundation for future applications of quantum-enhanced diagnostics with existing quantum simulators as well as emerging hardware.

title: Lineage Diffusion Models
authors: Bahman Moraffah, Raha Moraffah
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Latent variables often depend on directed graph structure. Many existing models impose simple trees or local independence, which limits their ability to represent uncertainty and long-range relationships under noise or missing data. These restrictions reduce the reliability of latent-state inference on general directed acyclic graphs. Lineage Diffusion Models (LDM) define a continuous diffusion process over latent variables placed on the nodes of a known DAG. A graph neural network approximates the reverse-time score so that the model supports structured, uncertainty-aware Bayesian inference on graph-conditioned latent spaces. The method handles multi-parent structure, incomplete observations, and variable graph density. Experiments show that LDM achieves higher inference accuracy and stronger uncertainty calibration than hierarchical Bayesian and graph-VAE baselines.

title: Unpaired Image Translation Using Cycle-Consistent GANs for the Development of a Digital Artwork Restoration System
authors: Adesh Kumar Mishra, S. Goshteeswaran, Aashim Dhawan, S Gowri, Naveen N M, Neha Jaswani
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: The paper suggests an effective strategy of digital art restoration grounded on unpaired image-to-image translation through CycleGANs (Cycle-Consistent Generative Adversarial Networks). The model uses a two-GAN based mapping between the damaged and restored artworks without using paired images dataset. To improve the semantic reconstruction, the generator is brought up with two attention mechanisms, namely, spatial attention and channel attention. Training: new loss functions: smooth L1-based perceptual cycle consistency loss, identity loss, and SSIM loss, using VGG-19 feature embeddings as optimisation target. Two datasets of 1349, 2041 artwork images were experimentally evaluated. The model presented in this paper has gained the best restoration results with MSE of 68.99, SSIM of 0.69 and PSNR of 30.88 on the 'Art images' dataset compared to CycleGAN (MSE: 77.11, SSIM: 0.60, PSNR: 29.54) and Pix2Pix. Also, the model achieved MSE: 104.91, SSIM: 0.54, PSNR: 27.94 on the 'Best artwork of all time information set. Visual and quantitative comparisons affirm that much better details preservation and textures restoration are achieved. The method proposed shall further automated restoration of artwork using context aware learning in GANs.

title: Brain-DGCNN: A Multi-Scale Dynamic Graph Neural Network for Parkinson's Disease Diagnosis
authors: Ruizhe Zhong, Huan Jiang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Early diagnosis of Parkinson's disease (PD) is an important prerequisite so that effective clinical interventions can be provided timely. Although graph neural network (GNN)-based models have recently demonstrated their potential advantage in analyzing resting-state fMRI, existing GNN methods all take the fixed graphs built upon static threshold as input without considering the adaptive nature characteristics of functional connections. In view of this problem, here we present a new Brain fMRI-Based Dynamic Graph Convolutional Network (Brain-DGCNN) framework for predicting whether the subjects belong to PD and HC, respectively. Different from previous studies based on fixed graph structures, our proposed Brain-DGCNN does not need any predefined adjacency matrices. Instead, we use dynamic edge convolution to rebuild topology structures using k-nearest neighbors in feature spaces layer by layer, which could well discover intrinsic long-distance functional connections. Experiments show that our method outperforms the compared baselines with an accuracy of 95.16%, F1-score of 95.15%, and AUC-ROC of 99%. Our result shows that it is beneficial to learn the topology dynamically rather than fix it in advance when predicting PD from fMRI data, providing a new idea for the accurate diagnosis of PD.

title: A Hybrid Approach to Classify Sentences for Instructional Content Identification in Sinhala
authors: S.A.R. Nethmini, Lochandaka Ranathunga
publish: 2025 IEEE 19th International Conference on Industrial and Information Systems (ICIIS)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid sentence classification framework for instructional content in the Sinhala language. It combines rule-based categorization, using domain-adapted bags of words and linguistic features, with a machine learning approach using sentence embeddings and logistic regression. The system efficiently distinguishes and filters out non-instructional sentences such as introductions, engagement phrases, and tips, while reliably identifying instructional steps. Experimental evaluation demonstrates the effectiveness of this strategy in accurately identifying and filtering instructional sentences in Sinhala text, forming a foundation for future applications in automated content organization and analysis. The approach was evaluated against instructional video-based content and achieved about 95.34% performance accuracy.

title: A Bayesian Multiobject Tracking Approach with Neural-Enhanced Motion and Measurement Models
authors: Shaoxiu Wei, Mingchao Liang, Florian Meyer
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: This paper introduces a hybrid Bayesian multiobject tracking (MOT) method that relies on a statistical model and makes use of neural networks to enhance parts of the model that have been identified as overly simplistic. Contrary to previous work, neural enhancement is performed for both the measurement and the motion model. To ensure tractable computation, our framework uses belief propagation (BP) to avoid high-dimensional operations combined with sequential Monte Carlo methods to perform low-dimensional operations efficiently. The resulting MOT method combines the flexibility and robustness of model-based approaches with the capability to learn from data of neural networks. We evaluate the performance of the proposed method based on the nuScenes autonomous driving dataset and demonstrate that it has state-of-the-art performance.

title: Gaze for Dyads & Blinks for Triads: Task Engagement Predictors in Free-Flow VR Interactions
authors: Carlos Gonzalez Diaz, Georgiana Cristina Dobre, Elisabeth André, Cristina Conati
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: This paper investigates in an exploratory manner small group interactions in Social VR classroom conversations using eye-tracking data (group eye-gaze configurations, blink rate and blink duration) in terms of task engagement. Task engagement has been previously studied in educational settings as it is an important construct for learning. Prior research showed that eye signals in groups can assess rapport, attention, agreement or communication engagement. However, little research focused on whether eye-tracking data can predict task engagement in educational small group Social VR conversations. We conducted an exploratory study with 52 pedagogy university students (8 dyads, 12 triads) engaging in free-flow conversations on a given topic. Results indicated that eye-tracking data could predict task engagement using multiple linear regression analysis. However, group eye-gaze configurations were the significant predictors in dyads, whereas the blink rate was the strongest predictor associated with task engagement in triads. Based on these findings, we discuss the higher complexity in triadic gaze dynamics and why the blink rate was the task engagement predictor. On the other hand, the simplicity of dyadic gaze configurations might as well explain why it was found as a predictor. Furthermore, we argue that future work can explore machine learning detectors of task engagement dependent of group size. The results from this exploratory study help understanding non-verbal group interactions in VR and contribute to further work on engagement models for more socially-abled virtual experiences in Social VR applications.

title: Intelligent AI-Based Service Provisioning and Task Offloading for Fog-Cloud Environment
authors: Swathi Pacha, Meenu, Prem Ranjan, Amit Kumar Dubey, Vishwa Nath Sharma, Ritu Sharma
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: In the era of Internet of Things (IoT) and the cumulative request for real-time services, the integration of Fog and Cloud computing has emerged as a promising solution for efficient resource management and task offloading. This paper presents an intelligent AI-based service provisioning and task offloading framework for Fog-Cloud environments. The proposed model leverages machine learning techniques to dynamically allocate resources, optimize task distribution, and reduce latency, thereby enhancing the overall system performance. By intelligently balancing computational loads between the Fog and Cloud layers, the framework ensures low energy consumption and high scalability while maintaining service quality. The system also incorporates context-aware decision-making mechanisms that adapt to varying workloads and user demands, ensuring efficient use of available resources. Simulation results demonstrate significant improvements in response time, energy efficiency, and resource utilization compared to traditional methods, making this approach highly suitable for delay-sensitive and resource-intensive applications.

title: Coarray DOA Estimation with Sparse Arrays Using Variable Window Size Spatial Smoothing
authors: Wesley S. Leite, Rodrigo C. de Lamare, Yuriy Zakharov, Wei Liu, Martin Haardt
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: In this work, we introduce a variable window size (VWS) spatial smoothing framework that enhances coarray-based direction of arrival (DOA) estimation for sparse linear arrays. By compressing the smoothing aperture, the proposed VWS Coarray MUSIC (VWS-CA-MUSIC) and VWS Coarray root-MUSIC (VWS-CA-rMUSIC) algorithms replace part of the perturbed rank-one outer products in the smoothed coarray data with unperturbed low-rank additional terms, increasing the separation between signal and noise subspaces, while preserving the signal subspace span. We also derive the bounds that guarantees identifiability, by limiting the values that can be assumed by the compression parameter. Simulations with sparse geometries reveal significant performance improvements and complexity savings relative to the fixed-window coarray MUSIC method.

title: Bias-Enhanced Successive Cancellation List Decoder for Polar Codes
authors: Jiajie Li, Sihui Shen, Warren J. Gross
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Polar codes with the successive cancellation (SC) de-coder are proven to achieve the channel capacity asymptotically. However, polar codes with SC decoding suffer from performance degradation when decoding short-to-medium length polar codes. The successive cancellation list (SCL) decoder improves the decoding performance of the SC decoder when decoding short-to-medium-length polar codes. The SCL decoder faces many implementation challenges, such as the decoding latency and the area overhead. A recently proposed perturbation-enhanced (PE) SCL decoder with a list size of L reaches the error correction performance of the SCL decoder with a list size of 2L. However, the reason behind this improved decoding performance needs to be investigated. An ablation study on the PE SCL decoder is performed in this work, and we found that the bias added to the received soft information is key for improved error correction performance. Hence, we propose to remove the random noise added to the PE SCL decoder, and we call our simplified decoder the bias-enhanced decoder. From experimental results, our bias-enhanced SCL decoder returns similar decoding performance to the PE SCL decoder under various code lengths and rates.

title: An Optimized Multidimensional Cloud Load Migration Method Based on Three-Way Decision
authors: Xinyu Ma, Jianxin Gai
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Dynamic changes in cloud load become a significant challenge for cloud resource management today. To address this issue, a three-way decision enhanced strategy for multidimensional cloud load migration control (MLCM-3WD) is proposed in this paper. First, in the virtual machine selection stage, the virtual machine migration control method is combined with the idea of three-way decision. The virtual machine multidimensional resource consumption rate (MRCR) is divided into three parts, and the set of virtual machines with small MRCR is selected. Then, in the VM placement phase, the improved single-step reinforcement learning strategy is used to place the set of VMs with low MRCR onto the physical machine with the highest multidimensional resource balance, completing the VM migration process. Finally, experiments are conducted on the CloudSimPlus platform. The results show that the proposed migration strategy can more effectively reduce energy consumption while controlling the number of virtual machine migrations.

title: DRaGon: Robust Emitter Geolocation via Deep Learning with NTP-Synchronized RTL-SDR Receivers
authors: Zulhaidir Hamid, Elyas Palantei, Intan Sari Areni
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Accurate radio frequency (RF) geolocation is vital for spectrum management, public safety, and defense. The Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA) method is effective but requires sub-nanosecond synchronization, traditionally achievable only with costly GPS-disciplined hardware. In contrast, the Network Time Protocol (NTP) over public networks have millisecond-level jitter, making it not suitable for conventional TDoA. This paper introduces DRaGon (Distributed Radio Geolocation), a deep learning framework that bypasses explicit time-difference estimation by learning an end-to-end mapping from raw in-phase and quadrature (I/Q) samples to geographic coordinates. Using three low-cost RTL-SDR receivers synchronized solely by NTP over cellular networks, we trained and evaluated DRaGon on a real-world dataset of more than $\mathbf{1 9 8, 0 0 0 ~ F M}$ broadcast samples collected in Semarang, Indonesia. Results show that DRaGon achieves a mean absolute localization error of 426 meters, a 63.24% improvement over sophisticated RSSI-based trilateration baselines. These findings challenge the assumption that precise hardware synchronization is indispensable for TDoA and demonstrate that synchronization errors and multipath effects can be treated as part of a learnable $\mathbf{R F}$ fingerprint, enabling affordable and scalable RF geolocation systems.

title: Personalised and Secured Melanoma Diagnosis Using Meta-Learning Driven Decentralised Framework
authors: Sudha D., R. Naresh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Imperative necessity in the healthcare systems is of compelling need to be addressed for earlier detection and diagnosis, where Melanoma. In this dermis-based cancer, early detection saves the patient from invasive treatments. But the stages of melanoma develop at a higher rate, it becomes the deadliest one as melanoma is the most lethal cancer. The growing Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) helps to collect patient data and sends it to the hospital rapidly. To maintain privacy and data integrity, the decentralised system based on a Federated learning system is an efficient way of communicating data in the network. But it lacks in personalisation and is affected by model poisoning attacks. To overcome such issues, the proposed work uses a meta-learningbased Federated learning model that updates the local patient and hospital client based on the meta gradient updates, making the system more dynamic and reducing attacks as the models' updates change continuously. Based on this, the proposed framework was simulated using the PH2 and ISIC 2020 datasets, yielding a 89% Global AUROC. When the proposed work was poisoned by 30%, an 85% Global AUROC was achieved.

title: Proceedings
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Transformative Computing Technologies (ICTCT)

title: AI-Based Risk Profiling of Online Human Trafficking Victims: A Multimodal Framework for Proactive Detection
authors: Aleena Ealias, Bhuvana Jayabalan, Mohana Priya
publish: 2025 International Conference on Transformative Computing Technologies (ICTCT)
abstract: Human trafficking is still a major worldwide crime that is made easier by digital channels, including social media, job advertisements, and websites that offer escort services. This study offers a thorough examination of artificial intelligence (AI) approaches to human trafficking detection and prevention, with a focus on identifying the most susceptible groups. From authorship attribution and geolocation extraction to social network analysis and multimodal detection, we analyze around 20 scholarly papers that include text, photos, audio, and network data. Lack of victim-focused profiling, data scarcity, bias in AI, and ethical deployment issues are some of the main research needs. In response, we provide a conceptual AI system that uses publicly available signals to evaluate individual vulnerability by combining network analysis, natural language processing, and weakly supervised learning. This work emphasizes the importance of ethically grounded AI systems to assist NGOs, law enforcement, and policymakers proactively identify at-risk individuals and prevent exploitation before it occurs.

title: An Experimental Study on Effects of Lighting Conditions for Better-Performance and Comfortable Gameplay
authors: Taiga Akiyama, Keita Ushida
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: In this paper, the authors researched player performance and mental and physical strain in gameplay under different lighting conditions through an experiment. Ten participants played three game titles under four different lighting conditions. Their gameplay was recorded, and the participants answered the survey about their mental and physical strain. No differences in player performance were observed across the four lighting conditions. However, the participants reported feeling more comfortable and experiencing less strain when playing in a well-lit room than in a dark one.

title: Future-Ready Charging for Light Electric Mobility: Techno-Economic Assessment of LEVDC Chargers for 2W and 3W
authors: V. Hemant Kumar, Yugal Kishor, Trilok Deshmukh
abstract: Electrification of two and three wheelers (2 W and 3W) offers India a defining opportunity to achieve sustainable and scalable urban and rural mobility. The full realization of light electric vehicle (LEV) adoption hinges on cost-effective, rapid, and reliable charging infrastructure. This paper undertakes a comprehensive techno-economic assessment of the new generation LEVDC (Light Electric Vehicle Direct Current) charging standard, with a focus on its suitability in India's unique market context for 2 W and 3 W segments. The analysis contrasts LEVDC fast chargers and conventional offboard AC slow chargers on technical, economic, and operational grounds, synthesizing recent policy frameworks, adoption data, and economic models. The study identifies key challenges and offers detailed recommendations for policy frameworks and market interventions to accelerate the rollout of future-ready LEV charging infrastructure across India's diverse regions.

title: Decentralized Vehicle to Infrastructure Communication and Data Exchange Using Proof of Stake
authors: Vairam T, Mukilan A
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: A growing requirement for intelligent transportation systems (ITS) and vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication provides the foundation for a safe, highly-scalable, and decentralized tolling and real-time data exchange solution. Blockchain solutions typically incorporate the overhead burden of using consensus algorithms such as PBFT, which do not provide the scalability required in dynamic vehicular environments. This thesis proposes a decentralized V2I communication and data exchange mechanism, based on using a Proof of Stake (PoS) Blockchain.
