The enhanced gravity model incorporates factors such as population size, road density, and multimodal accessibility to better capture spatial mobility dynamics in a developing megacity context. The results indicate that neither population size nor road availability alone explains ride-hailing mobility patterns. Correlation analysis reveals moderately positive relationships between road concentration and trip interactions, while unique patterns emerge in densely populated but low-mobility areas, as well as in transit-oriented zones showing high interaction despite limited road infrastructure. These findings highlight the significance of integrating infrastructure, accessibility, and land use considerations to support balanced and sustainable urban mobility planning in Jakarta.

title: Intrusion Response System Using Reinforcement Learning TD3 Algorithm
authors: Dharani Kanta Roy, Hemanta Kumar Kalita
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: The increasing complexity of cyberattacks requires intelligent intrusion response systems that provide adaptive defense. This paper introduces a novel Intrusion Response System (IRS) that utilizes the Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm, an advanced reinforcement learning technique suited for continuous action spaces. Unlike traditional intrusion detection methods that focus solely on identifying threats, our system proactively learns optimal defense strategies to minimize damage and further recovery. In modeling the cybersecurity environment as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), a hierarchical state representation is employed that captures multilevel network contexts, enabling a detailed understanding of emerging threats. To improve decision-making, integrate a Feature-wise Additive Attention mechanism that highlights the most critical features in the network state, enabling the TD3 agent to focus on relevant information when selecting responses. The response is optimized by training the TD3 agent's attention and a multi-objective reward function, enabling the system to dynamically learn adaptive strategies that minimize damage, reduce false alarms, and accelerate recovery. The system is implemented in MATLAB using cybersecurity dataset UNSW-NB15, which includes a range of single-stage and multi-stage attack scenarios. TD3 reinforcement learning agent, attention mechanisms, and a multi-objective reward function to minimize damage, speed up recovery, and maintain system stability, thereby responding intelligently to confirmed threats.

title: Multi-Modal Sensor Fusion for Proactive Blockage Prediction in mmWave Vehicular Networks
authors: Ahmad M. Nazar, Abdulkadir Celik, Mohamed Y. Selim, Asmaa Abdallah, Daji Qiao, Ahmed M. Eltawil
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Vehicular communication systems operating in the millimeter wave (mmWave) band are highly susceptible to signal blockage from dynamic obstacles such as vehicles, pedestrians, and infrastructure. To address this challenge, we propose a proactive blockage prediction framework that utilizes multimodal sensing, including camera, GPS, LiDAR, and radar inputs in an infrastructure-to-vehicle (I2V) setting. This approach uses modality-specific deep learning models to process each sensor stream independently and fuses their outputs using a softmax-weighted ensemble strategy based on validation performance. Our evaluations, for up to 1.5s in advance, show that the camera-only model achieves the best standalone trade-off with an F1-score of 97.1% and an inference time of 89.8ms. A camera+radar configuration further improves accuracy to 97.2% F1 at 95.7ms. Our results display the effectiveness and efficiency of multi-modal sensing for mmWave blockage prediction and provide a pathway for proactive wireless communication in dynamic environments.

title: Multivariate EMD for EEG-Based Characterization of Neurodegenerative Diseases
authors: Ethan Zhu, Marjan Nassajpour, Behnaz Ghoraani
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Accurate and early detection of neurodegenerative diseases using electroencephalography (EEG) remains a challenging yet vital task in clinical neuroscience. This paper presents a data-driven framework for EEG signal decomposition and feature extraction using Multivariate Empirical Mode Decomposition (MEMD), aiming to enhance classification and spatial analysis of EEG signals in patients with neurodegenerative disorders. EEG recordings from healthy controls (HC), Alzheimer’s disease (AD), and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) subjects were processed through both MEMD and conventional Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD). A comprehensive set of temporal and spectral features was extracted from intrinsic mode functions (IMFs), and statistically significant features were used to train support vector machines (SVM), Random Forest, and XGBoost classifiers. Our results demonstrate that MEMD yields superior classification accuracy when compared to EMD, achieving the best results with XGBoost for AD vs. HC (0.83 vs. 0.71) and AD vs. FTD (0.76 vs. 0.70), and with SVM for FTD vs. HC (0.77 vs. 0.70). These findings highlight the potential of MEMD as a robust EEG processing tool for characterizing neural changes associated with neurodegenerative diseases.

title: Directional Modulation: Advancing Electromagnetic Signal Processing for Secure Communications
authors: G. Bacci, G. Galgani, R. Guidi, M. Luise, V. Pellegrini, L. Sanguinetti
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Improving security features of wireless communications for both civil and military applications has been a hot topic since the early days of radio science. One effective method is through physical-layer (PHY) encryption, that can be achieved through the application of advanced electromagnetic signal processing techniques at both transmitting and receiving link-ends by using antenna arrays, possibly in a disjoint manner. Among others, directional modulation (DM) can be regarded as a promising technique, which uses antenna arrays as spatial encryption systems, to provide information confidentiality in the transmit direction, already at the PHY. Still, as securing communications extends beyond countering eavesdropping by also comprising integrity, availability and reliability guarantees for the transmitted information, it turns out that PHY approaches can help at the receive link-end with mitigation of such attacks as jamming, meaconing, and spoofing. This can be achieved through antenna arrays with dynamic phase shifting, and can enhance communication, radar, and navigation applications. While there exists a consistent literature for the transmit-side, to the best of our knowledge similar solutions at the receiver side are not available. In this paper we describe a DM-based approach to design an add-on antenna array that partitions the surrounding space into intelligible and interdicted zones. Within the latter, any harmful signal originating from potential attackers is rejected by taking advantage of traditional beamforming techniques, such as null-steering, while also building upon them via the application of the space-dependent distortion processes associated with DM.

title: Natural Language Processing for Healthcare Text Mining and Clinical Decision Support
authors: Abhinav Mishra, Sakshi Sobti, M. Gowri, P.S. Joan Kingsly, T Pushpalatha, N. Kalidas
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Smart Innovations in Systems, Infrastructure, Mechanical, Power, AI and Computing Technologies (SISIMPACT)
abstract: Natural Language Processing (NLP) has developed a powerful technique of obtaining valuable knowledge out of the enormous volume of unstructured textual information produced within healthcare systems in clinical notes, discharged summaries, medical literature, etc. This paper discusses some of the advanced NLP text mining methods in healthcare with a view to supporting accurate and expedient clinical decision making. The suggested framework combines the models of domain-specific languages, semantics and named entity recognition to detect key medical concepts, discover patterns, and obtain contextual relationships. Using the ontologies and medical knowledge bases enables the system to maximize information search and facilitate evidence-based medical decision-making. The strategy will ensure the minimization of diagnosis mistakes, enhance treatment planning, and help healthcare specialists control the patient care process in a more efficient manner. Experimental evaluation of the NLP-based text mining value, promise, in optimizing workflows, individualization of care, and proactive interventions is evidence of how intelligent, data-driven, scalable, and saleable healthcare solutions can be achieved.

title: Analytic Comparison of Super-Resolution Artificial Intelligence Models: UAE Case Study for Upscaling Digital Elevation Models
authors: Ahmed Ammar Kuba, Mohammed Ahmed, Qassim Nasir, Mohammed Kamil, Manar Abu Talib
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Access to high-resolution terrain data, which is important for a wide range of geospatial applications, remains limited in the gulf region. Estimating high-resolution Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) from low-resolution maps can be a viable approach to help address this problem. This research study investigates the use of deep learning-based super-resolution techniques to enhance low-resolution DEMs in the context of the United Arab Emirates (UAE). We evaluate four artificial intelligence upscaling models for recovering high-resolution elevation details from UAE DEMs. The models are trained on a dataset consisting of 97 high- and low-resolution DEM pairs, with the high-resolution maps having a resolution of 1 meter, and 10 meters for the low-resolution maps. The models are evaluated using metrics like PSNR, RMSE, and MAE. VDSR4DEM achieved the highest accuracy with a PSNR of 69.0 dB, RMSE of 1.482 meters, and MAE of 0.864 meters, outperforming D-SRGAN (PSNR 41.9, RMSE 1.752, MAE 1.257), SRCNN (PSNR 54.14, RMSE 6.78, MAE 5.73), and Real-ESRGAN (PSNR 30.0, RMSE 7.03, MAE 5.92). VDSR can upscale DEMs without image conversion, minimizing artifacts and preserving terrain integrity. These findings demonstrate the potential of AI super-resolution to generate high-quality elevation data for geospatial analysis in arid and urban regions. Potential future directions include training more advanced super-resolution models on UAE DEMs to improve accuracy, using 3D visualization tools for better analysis and planning, and integrating upscaled UAE DEMs into flood modeling and hydrological simulations.

title: Geospatial Modeling of House Price Using MGWRSAR: A Case Study in DKI Jakarta
authors: Yenro P Sagala, Sutikno, Achmad Choiruddin
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: The dynamics of house prices in Jakarta are influenced by both spatial dependence and spatial heterogeneity. Conventional models, including the Spatial Autoregressive (SAR) and Geographically Weighted Regression (GWR), are only capable of partial capture of these effects, which often result in model misspecification. The present study employs the Mixed Geographically Weighted Regression-Spatial Autoregressive (MGWR-SAR) model to concurrently address the spatial dependence and heterogeneity in house price determinants. We used a dataset of houses listed in 2024, incorporating house and location characteristics. The construction of spatial dependence weight matrix was based on semi-variogram analysis, and optimal kernel bandwidths were selected using cross-validation method. The model is estimated using The Spatial Two-Stage Least Square (S2SLS) approach to address endogeneity in spatial lag component. The findings of the study indicate that the land and building area are the most significant price determinants, with spatial variation in their influence across Jakarta’s subregions. Jakarta Pusat exhibits stronger spatial spillovers, while Southern district demonstrates heightened sensitivity to building area. The MGWR-SAR model effectively eliminates residual spatial autocorrelation and captures location-specific variation, offering enhanced explanatory power for urban housing market. These findings underscore the importance of incorporating spatial structure in housing price modeling for more targeted policies and valuation strategies.

title: Armed Unmanned Ground Vehicle Using Virtual Reality
authors: Swathi K, Nutana Shetty, Akshatha N M, Shrinidhi Alva
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Recent Trends in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technologies (ICRTEECT)
abstract: The design and deployment of an Armed Virtual Reality-Based Unmanned Ground Vehicle (AVR-UGV) are examined in this technical study. In a variety of applications, such as military operations, long-distance transportation, hazardous environment investigation, and surveillance, AVR-UGVs provide notable advantages over human operation. This study combines a gyroscope sensor with a VR headset to record head motions, which are subsequently sent to a UGV microcontroller. After processing this data, the microcontroller rotates a camera in three axes using servo motors, giving the VR headset real-time video input. The operator can experience and operate the UGV in a virtual reality setting thanks to this configuration. The UGV also has a robotic arm that can move like a human hand, allowing for remote object manipulation.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Joint Beamforming and Channel Tracking via Sparse Bayesian Filtering for Massive MIMO
authors: Xichun Cheng, Risto Wichman
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: The temporal variation of massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) channels poses significant challenges for accurate channel estimation and effective beamforming design. To address this problem, we propose an end-to-end deep learning scheme for channel tracking with beamforming optimization. The proposed scheme integrates a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based beamformer fed by the sparse channel estimates to align with the dominant directions, together with an adaptive channel estimator using the online variational sparse Bayesian filter (OVSBF) algorithm, which recursively updates the channel information at each time slot by leveraging temporal correlations. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves superior beam alignment and significantly outperforms other beamforming benchmarks.

title: Performance Analysis of RIS-Aided Synchronized Systems
authors: Hadi Hariri, Nazih Salhab
publish: 2025 International Conference on Innovation and Intelligence for Informatics, Computing, and Technologies (3ICT)
abstract: The uncontrollable nature of radio wave propagation poses a significant challenge to wireless network performance. As a promising solution, Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) can intelligently reconfigure the propagation environment by shaping and directing radio waves. In this paper, we investigate an RIS-assisted uplink communication system in which a user communicates with a base station (BS) via an RIS. We propose a mathematical model for the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), outage probability, and data throughput under Nakagamim fading, with a specific focus on the critical impact of phase synchronization. Then we formulate our optimization problem with the aim of maximizing the achievable rate. Our results demonstrate that synchronized RIS phase shifts lead to a notable improvement in received SNR and a significant reduction in outage probability. Specifically, an interestingly low outage probability is achievable at a transmit power of 15 dBm with phase synchronization, highlighting the substantial gains over both non-synchronized RIS or conventional communication without using an RIS. Our analysis of these three scenarios—with synchronized RIS, non-synchronized RIS, and without RIS confirms the pivotal role of RIS phase synchronization for optimizing performance.

title: Target Induced Angle Grid Regularized Estimation for Ghost Identification in Automotive Radar
authors: Junho Kweon, Vishal Monga
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: This study presents a novel algorithm for identifying ghost targets in automotive radar by estimating complex valued signal strength across a two-dimensional angle grid defined by direction-of-arrival (DOA) and direction-of-departure (DOD). In real-world driving environments, radar signals often undergo multipath propagation due to reflections from surfaces such as guardrails. These indirect paths can produce ghost targets—false detections that appear at incorrect locations—posing challenges to autonomous navigation. A recent method, the Multi-Path Iterative Adaptive Approach (MP-IAA), addresses this by jointly estimating the DOA/DOD angle grid, identifying mismatches as indicators of ghost targets. However, its effectiveness declines in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) settings. To enhance robustness, we introduce a physics-inspired regularizer that captures structural patterns inherent to multipath propagation. This regularizer is incorporated into the estimation cost, forming a new loss function that guides our proposed algorithm, TIGRE (Target-Induced angle-Grid Regularized Estimation). TIGRE iteratively minimizes this regularized loss and we show that our proposed regularizer asymptotically enforces l<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf> sparsity on the DOA/DOD grid. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed method substantially enhances the quality of angle-grid estimation across various multipath scenarios, particularly in low SNR environments, providing a more reliable basis for subsequent ghost target identification.

title: Robust Training of Low-Parameter Models for Active Speaker Detection from Digital Videos
authors: Sebastian Janampa, Bonnie Morales Ugarte, Constantinos S. Pattichis, Marios S. Pattichis
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: Active speaker detection (ASD) uses visual and audio information from raw videos to determine which speakers are talking in each frame. The majority of models for ASD have been large-parameter models. Recently, Light-ASD and LR-ASD achieved a reduction of 41X in the number of required parameters while also achieving competitive performance. Yet, during training, Light-ASD and LR-ASD can often suffer from vanishing gradients. In this paper, we modify the original architecture to avoid the vanishing gradient problem while reducing the required number of parameters and FLOPs. In out-of-distribution testing, our proposed model surpasses all competitors while using fewer parameters and FLOPs.

title: Optimization of Coalition Update Mechanisms for Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Jie Zhang, Fengyuan Yang, Yingqi Liao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) achieve complex task solving through cooperation among individual agents and have been widely applied in swarm intelligence, cooperative control, and distributed decision-making. As an important organizational form of multi-agent cooperation, the coalition structure plays a critical role in determining overall system performance and cooperative efficiency, and its dynamic update mechanism directly affects system effectiveness. However, existing coalition update methods generally rely on static rules or local rewarddriven strategies, making it difficult to balance global cooperative benefits and system stability in complex dynamic environments. To address this issue, this paper proposes an optimization method for coalition update mechanisms in multi-agent systems. The proposed method constructs a coalition utility function and introduces individual contribution evaluation and structural evolution constraints to achieve adaptive optimization of coalition structures while ensuring system stability. Furthermore, by combining game-theoretic principles with heuristic search strategies, an efficient coalition update strategy is designed to reduce computational complexity and improve convergence performance. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms comparative approaches in terms of cooperative reward, system stability, and convergence speed, validating its effectiveness and robustness in multi-agent cooperative tasks.

title: Sentinel-5P Image and HYSPLIT Modeling for Analysis The Distribution of Sulfur Dioxide and The Impact on Acute Respiratory Infection in Surabaya City
authors: Shafa Nabila Prawitasari, Bangun Muljo Sukojo
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Urbanization in Surabaya has led to increased air pollution from vehicle emissions and industrial activities, with significant impacts on public health and air quality. Sulfur Dioxide (SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) emissions are one of the factors contributing to the high incidence of Acute Respiratory Infections (ARI). Integration of Sentinel-5P with HYSPLIT modeling is needed to fill data gaps and enhance the accuracy, analysis and efficiency. Sentinel-5P SO2 for periods 2020-2023 were processed using the Google Earth Engine platform and HYSPLIT model was used to simulate the transport and dispersion of pollutants in the atmosphere. The results indicate that SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> concentrations in Surabaya show fluctuations each year. However, ISPU in Surabaya City is in the good category between 2020-2023. Correlation between SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> and ARI shows a weak relationship between 0,091 and 0,2524. Spatial analysis shows a stronger relationship in each subdistrict with an $\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$ value of 0.008 to 0,795. The simulation from HYSPLIT shows the particle trajectory and SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> concentration distribution, indicating a spread toward the north to northeast with a range up to 50 km and pollution sources near the surface, such as factories and textile industries. This research shows the spatial and temporal distribution of SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> and potential impacts on ARI. Therefore, this research is expected to be considered in the reformulation of air pollution control and prevention.

title: Pyramid Broad Learning System for Object and Scene Point Cloud Segmentation
authors: Yifei Tian, Yan Fu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Big Data and Data Mining (BDDM)
abstract: Point clouds, known for their high precision and spatial richness, play a crucial role in 3D scene understanding and object-level analysis. However, their unordered and irregular structure presents significant challenges for direct feature extraction and segmentation using traditional models. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a novel variant of the broad learning system, termed the pyramid broad network (PyramidBLS), designed specifically for efficient and accurate 3D point cloud segmentation. The proposed model incorporates a multi-scale feature mapping layer to capture detailed point-wise geometric features. These features are enhanced in the nonlinear enhancement layer by projecting them into a high-dimensional space, enabling better representation. Furthermore, a pyramid structure integrates local contextual information at multiple receptive field scales with varying neighborhood sizes. By concatenating outputs from the feature, enhancement, and pyramid layers, the architecture forms a broad and shallow network where all units directly contribute to the final segmentation output. To enhance adaptability and scalability, PyramidBLS supports horizontal expansion by adding more enhancement nodes and vertical stacking by deploying multiple broad networks in series. The model is evaluated on two benchmark datasets: ShapeNet for object part segmentation and S3DIS for indoor scene semantic segmentation. Experimental results demonstrate that PyramidBLS achieves superior performance compared to existing broad learning algorithms and machine learning models, highlighting its effectiveness in complex point cloud segmentation tasks.

title: Convergence Assurance for Probabilistic AI: Rank-Aware, Tail-Sensitive Diagnostics for Multi-Chain Inference
authors: Keval Barvaliya
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Trends in Computing and Communication (ETCOM)
abstract: Reliable uncertainty in modern AI systems hinges on robust Bayesian inference, yet standard convergence checks can miss scale and heavy-tail pathologies in multi-chain samplers; this paper introduces a rank-aware convergence suite that fuses rank-normalized and folded <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\widehat{R}$</tex> with bulk/tail effective sample sizes and rank-based visual diagnostics to detect location, scale, and tail mismatches across chains in probabilistic pipelines. The methods provide actionable thresholds and quantile-focused Monte Carlo error estimates, and are validated via controlled simulations and canonical case studies, showing improved sensitivity over traditional diagnostics while remaining practical for large-scale probabilistic programming used in AI.

title: Scenario-Based Liquefaction Potential Analysis Using Geotechnical Data and Electrical Resistivity Tomography: A Case Study in Caraga State University, Butuan City, Philippines
authors: Rutchen A. Alibangbang, Hizle Jeyhne J. Busque, Olayvar Yvonne T, Daniel Jose L. Buhay, Nichole Anthony D. Pacle
publish: 2025 IEEE Asia-Pacific Conference on Geoscience, Electronics and Remote Sensing Technology (AGERS)
abstract: Butuan City, Philippines, is prone to seismic events due to its proximity to numerous earthquake generators, resulting in a range of hazards such as liquefaction. This study investigates the liquefaction potential in Caraga State University - Main Campus by analyzing the PF: Surigao and PF: Esperanza segments, an unnamed fault west of Butuan City, and the Philippine Trench. The maximum magnitudes Mw 7.4, Mw 7.1, Mw 6.5, and Mw 8.1, respectively, along with the minimum recorded Mw 5.1, which triggered liquefaction in the Philippines, were considered. A deterministic approach was employed to calculate ground attenuation values. Electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) profiles identified areas prone to liquefaction using resistivity meters, and borehole data were used to calculate the liquefaction potential index (LPI). The calculated LPI varies with the earthquake magnitude and PGA, ranging from high (5-15 LPI) to very high potential (>15 LPI) based on maximum magnitudes and low to no liquefaction based on the smallest magnitude. These findings underscore the need for effective mitigation strategies to reduce the potential damage caused by liquefaction in the study area.

title: Spectral regularization for adversarially-robust representation learning
authors: Sheng Yang, Jacob A. Zavatone-Veth, Cengiz Pehlevan
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: The vulnerability of neural network classifiers to adversarial attacks is a major obstacle to their deployment in safety-critical applications. Regularization of network parameters during training can be used to improve adversarial robustness and generalization performance. Usually, the network is regularized end-to-end, with parameters at all layers affected by regularization. However, in settings where learning representations is key, such as self-supervised learning (SSL), layers after the feature representation will be discarded when performing inference. For these models, regularizing up to the feature space is more suitable. To this end, we propose a new spectral regularizer for representation learning that encourages black-box adversarial robustness in downstream classification tasks. In supervised classification settings, we show empirically that this method is more effective in boosting test accuracy and robustness than previously-proposed methods that regularize all layers of the network. We then show that this method improves the adversarial robustness of classifiers using representations learned with self-supervised training or transferred from another classification task. In all, our work begins to unveil how representational structure affects adversarial robustness.

title: VR Panorama Inpainting: A Feasibility Study of High-Resolution View Synthesis for Virtual Reality
authors: Lillian Eagan, Nam Nguyen, Angela Victoria Chen, Theresa Zhu, Seth Maxwell Johnson, Pranav Reddy Dumpa, Benjamin Jonson Geil, Jonathan Ventura, Stefanie Zollmann
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and eXtended and Virtual Reality (AIxVR)
abstract: In this work, we propose a machine learning-based pipeline for generating a 3D-colored mesh representation of a scene from a single-input panorama. Our approach allows us to create a fully immersive Virtual Reality (VR) experiences. Unlike neural rendering approaches that are constrained by lowresolution inputs and offline processing, we focus on high-quality resolution inputs (4 K) and real-time rendering on VR headsets by estimating a depth map and creating a layered depth image (LDI) representation. We iteratively fill unseen regions behind objects through a cutting and inpainting process, after which the LDI is converted into an optimized, texture-mapped 3D mesh, resulting in a compact and efficient representation. We assess the feasibility of this approach through a quantitative evaluation with a user study in VR, focusing on the practicality, visual quality, and perceived presence of the generated scenes.

title: Charting Channels in the Presence of RIS
authors: Mohamed El Mehdi Makhlouf, Yamil Vindas, Anil Kumar, Maxime Guillaud, Marco Di Renzo
publish: 2025 59th Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems, and Computers
abstract: This work explores the extension of channel charting, a technique for constructing low-dimensional representations of channel state information (CSI) through self-supervised learning, to wireless environments including reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS). Channel charting has shown promise in pseudo-position based radio resource management applications by capturing spatial relationships between transmitters and receivers. We investigate the challenges and opportunities that RISs present to channel charting, particularly focusing on how RIS reconfiguration affects the learned low-dimensional representations. We focus on a contrastive learning framework with triplet loss to derive a channel chart through distance learning, and incorporate the RIS configuration information into the learning process, aiming to develop channel charts that are robust to changes in the RIS configuration. We also explore the potential for leveraging channel charts to inform RIS configuration, enabling applications where the channel chart is used to determine optimal RIS settings from a given codebook.

title: Polymorphism and Distributivity Properties of MDE Model Refactorings
authors: N/A
abstract: None

title: Silent Guardians: Independent and Secure Decision Tree Evaluation Without Chatter_supp1-3675596.pdf
authors: Liang Feng Zhang
abstract: As machine learning as a service (MLaaS) gains increasing popularity, it raises two critical challenges: privacy and verifiability. For privacy, clients are reluctant to disclose sensitive private information to access MLaaS, while model providers must safeguard their proprietary models. For verifiability, clients lack reliable mechanisms to ensure that cloud servers execute model inference correctly. Decision trees are widely adopted in MLaaS due to their popularity, interpretability, and broad applicability in domains like medicine and finance. In this context, outsourcing decision tree evaluation (ODTE) enables both clients and model providers to offload their sensitive data and decision tree models to the cloud securely. However, existing ODTE schemes often fail to address both privacy and verifiability simultaneously. To bridge this gap, we propose PVODTE, a novel two-server private and verifiable ODTE protocol that leverages homomorphic secret sharing and a MAC-based verification mechanism. PVODTE eliminates the need for server-to-server communication, enabling independent computation by each cloud server. This “non-interactive” setting addresses the latency and synchronization bottlenecks of prior arts, making it uniquely suitable for wide-area network (WAN) deployments. To our knowledge, PVODTE is the first two-server ODTE protocol that eliminates server-to-server communication. Furthermore, PVODTE achieves security against malicious servers, where servers cannot learn anything about the client's input or the providers' decision tree models, and servers cannot alter the inference result without being detected.

title: ICRTEECT 2025 Preface
authors: N/A

title: Intelligent Monitoring and Analysis Algorithm for C17ORF49 Gene Expression in ERα-Positive Breast Cancer
authors: Liya Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: To address the dimensional limitations of traditional single-modal analysis in clinical decision-making for ERα-positive breast cancer, a multimodal feature fusion attention algorithm (MFFA) was proposed. This algorithm employs a four-layer progressive architecture. The input layer constructs a multidimensional system by selecting RNA-seq gene data (including C17ORF49), clinical pathology data, and pathology slide imaging features. The feature extraction layer processes gene and time-series data using an improved CNN and bidirectional LSTM, respectively. The attention fusion layer utilizes dynamic weighting to enhance key features. The output layer performs dual-task risk stratification and gene expression prediction in parallel. The experiment utilized dual-source data from the TCGA database (1200 cases) and a local hospital (300 cases). After preprocessing with KNN interpolation and 3σ outlier removal, the dataset was partitioned into a 7:1.5:1.5 split. Using NVIDIA A100 hardware, MFFA was compared with SVM, XGBoost, and a standard attention model. The results showed that MFFA achieved an Accuracy of 94.2% and an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.973 for classification tasks, representing improvements of 7.7% and 8.1% respectively over XGBoost. The RMSE for regression tasks was 0.086, a 43.4% reduction over the standard attention model. In a noise interference experiment, MFFA achieved an Accuracy of 89.5% even with 30% noise, and the AUC variation across datasets was only 1.5%, demonstrating its robustness and generalizability, providing support for precision diagnosis and treatment of breast cancer.

title: Emotion Recognition from Electroencephalogram Signals using Hybrid Neural Networks
authors: Arulmozhivarman Velusamy, Harish J, Angeline J, Karthika R, Shreyas P
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: Emotion recognition from electroencephalogram (EEG) signals plays a crucial role in advancing Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) systems, with applications in mental health monitoring, adaptive human-computer interaction, and affective computing. In this study, we investigate the performance of recurrent, convolutional, and hybrid deep learning architectures namely Gated Recurrent Units (GRU), Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) networks, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and their hybrid combinations for classifying human emotions from a publicly available EEG dataset. The proposed hybrid architecture leverages the temporal feature extraction capability of recurrent models and the spatial pattern recognition strength of CNNs. Experimental results demonstrate that the hybrid model achieves a classification accuracy of 96%, significantly outperforming individual model counterparts. PCA has been used to enhance the speed and reduce memory usage of our hybrid system, significantly improving its accuracy to 98% by limiting input feature set size. This is because when using several techniques in conjunction with PCA feature engineering, we can create a system that performs more predictably and dependably for emotion recognition from EEG data in real-world applications of BCI.

title: Remote-Operated Floating Garbage Collector for Surface Water Cleanup
authors: Prottay Mazumder, Supriya, Anusha, Muskaan Bharadwaj, Swati Swayamsiddha, Manas Ranjan Tripathy
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Communication and Computers (ELEXCOM)
abstract: The growing problem of surface water pollution, which is caused by industrial pollutants, floating debris, floating waste in rivers and lakes severely impacts aquatic habitats and human activities. This work describes the creation of a remotely operated device designed to gather floating debris from water surfaces. The prototype combines propulsion and conveyor mechanisms with wireless monitoring through ESP32 CAM and Blynk IoT, allowing real-time control and reduced manual effort.The system integrates BO motors for propulsion and conveyor-driven waste collection, efficiently gathering debris into a dedicated storage unit. Remote monitoring and control are facilitated by an ESP32 CAM module and the Blynk application, enabling real-time oversight and reducing the need for manual intervention. The structural framework, constructed from PVC and CPVC pipes, guarantees resilience, buoyancy, and versatility in a range of aquatic conditions. By leveraging automation and remote operation, the proposed system minimizes labor costs, enhances cleaning efficiency, and contributes to sustainable water management efforts. This project aligns with global environmental initiatives, such as supports initiatives focused on river rejuvenation and sustainable water cleanup. Promising avenues to tackle these issues are provided by recent developments in robotics, computer vision, Internet of Things (IoT) technologies, and cloud computing offer promising pathways to address these challenges. Design and implementation of this low cost IoT project implies sustainable use of these resources for real time water surface cleaning.

title: Stretching Effect Estimation Model Based on Surface Electromyography
authors: Takuma Asano, Teppei Araki, Hiroshi Kawaguchi, Shintaro Izumi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Static stretching is a fundamental conditioning practice; however, objectively and quantitatively evaluating its physiological effects remains a significant challenge. Conventional surface electromyography analysis has predominantly relied on single-channel derived indices, such as Root Mean Square and Median Frequency. However, relying solely on single scalar metrics is insufficient to comprehensively capture local variations in muscle activity and alterations in complex neuromuscular control, suggesting that the establishment of more multifaceted evaluation methodologies is valuable. In this study, we propose a novel stretching evaluation method based on spatiotemporal pattern recognition using 16-channel surface electromyography and deep learning, specifically aimed at application in consumer electronic devices such as wearable devices for personal fitness management. Specifically, scalograms generated by Continuous Wavelet Transform and information regarding the sensor's spatial arrangement are input into a Convolutional Neural Network to construct a model that classifies the degree of stretching. Experimental results with five healthy adults demonstrated that the proposed model achieved high classification performance, with an F1-score of 88.2%. Furthermore, visualization analysis of the feature space using t-SNE confirmed that data from an intermediate stretching duration, which was excluded from training, was continuously mapped to the intermediate region between the two classes. This suggests that the proposed model learns not only discrete class classification but also the continuous transition dynamics—reflecting the depth of physiological change—of the muscle state associated with stretching intervention. This method holds potential for application as an objective biomarker for defining optimal stretching durations tailored to individual muscle characteristics for next-generation personal training assistants.

title: Waste Heat Recovery from Power Electronic Systems Using Thermo Electric Modules
authors: Vivek R S, Divyamol S, Jayan Pp, Aby Joseph
abstract: Waste heat recovery presents a promising avenue for improving the energy efficiency of power electronic systems, where significant thermal energy is often dissipated without utilization. This research paper investigates the application of thermoelectric generator (TEG) modules for harnessing waste heat from the exhaust pipe of such systems. TEG modules, operating on the Seebeck effect, convert thermal gradients directly into electrical energy. In this study, a TEG module is subjected to a controlled temperature difference of 30 °C between an exhaust gas temperature of 60 °C and an ambient temperature of 30 °C. A simulation is performed to evaluate the thermal and electrical behavior of the system, focusing on the performance of a thermocouple embedded within the TEG module. The generated electrical output is assessed for its feasibility in powering a Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA). An analytical model is also developed to complement the numerical simulation, and the results from both methods are compared to validate the approach. The outcomes of this research highlight the potential of TEG-based waste heat recovery for power electronics

title: Predicting Maternal Health Risks Using Neural Networks and Feature Engineering
authors: P Loganathan, P Gowsikraja, P A Selvaraj, N D Shrinivassan, V Yashika, K Vijayakumar
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: A total of 1,014 maternal patients from a clinical setting developed a modeling framework for estimating maternal health risk incorporating physiological indicators of age, temperature, blood glucose, heart rate and blood pressure. Clinical indicators were distilled by advanced medical feature engineering to discover meaningful indicators such as blood pressure ratios, pulse pressure, mean arterial pressure, and a risk score for overall assessment. Maternal risk was examined and classified as low, mid, and high risk using ensemble machine learning methods (XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest and Gradient Boosting) and a modified neural network. Class imbalance was identified and addressed with the ADASYN approach, while an explainable AI approach using SHAP was applied to facilitate interpretation of the outcomes from both ensemble and deep learning models. The highest-performing method yielded 95% accuracy and F1 score of 0.95, suggesting that combining clinical feature engineering with ensemble and neural network methods of modeling maternal health risk may be effective.

title: Electrode-Level Association Analysis of EEG Connectivity in Children with ADHD Using Positive Pointwise Mutual Information
authors: Lakshmi Kalpana Roy K, Shreya Venkatesh, Malar B
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: Computational Neuroscience is an interdisciplinary field leveraging mathematical and computational methodologies to develop theoretical models for the diagnosis of various brain disorders via neural connectivity analysis. Positive Point-wise Mutual Information (PPMI), a non-linear measure traditionally used in Natural Language Processing, is introduced in this study as an innovative analytical tool for assessing functional brain connectivity in electroencephalography (EEG) data. This work discusses the application of PPMI in neurological signal analysis to characterize altered connectivity patterns in children diagnosed with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) compared with age-matched healthy controls. By quantifying the probability of strong associations between EEG electrode pairs, PPMI provides a robust framework for identifying connectivity alterations that conventional linear measures may overlook. The study employs multiple visualization methods-including line graphs to represent connectivity probabilities, bar graphs illustrating pairwise association strengths, network graphs depicting global organization, and topoplot-based spatial projections-to reveal region-specific connectivity deviations. Results demonstrate significant reorganization of functional networks in the ADHD group. Several electrodes, particularly Fp2, C3, C4, P3, and Fz, consistently exhibit elevated probabilities of forming strong associations, indicating localized hyperconnectivity. In contrast, evaluation of specific electrode pairings such as Fp1-Pz, F3-F7, and Fp2-F4 uncovers a complex interplay of both heightened and reduced connectivity, reflecting heterogeneous network disruptions. Overall, the findings highlight PPMI as a promising biomarker for capturing subtle yet meaningful connectivity variations in ADHD. Its capacity to elucidate non-linear functional interactions positions it as a valuable tool for advancing computational neurodiagnostics and improving understanding of ADHD-related neural dysregulation. c

title: Secure Human Identification with Multi-Modal Biometrics Using Weighted Jackal Optimization and Transformer-Based Squeezenet
authors: Vijay M, Golla Aravind Kumar, Ganji Umanageswar, Edamakanti Ramanji Reddy, Jangala Gurunadham, N Nandhini
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: The restrictions of unimodal systems have resulted in the introduction of multimodal biometric systems. The paper introduces a skeleton based fusion-based multimodal identification system which involves the use of fingerprint, palmprint and iris data. To start with, a guided non-local filter enhances the quality of the image whereas SiaNet is used to extract discriminative features. Parallel maximum covariance fusion then fuses multimodal embeddings, optimizing the best features using a jackal optimization strategy with weights. Lastly, a transformer based SqueezeNet classifier is used to perform recognition. Its system has 99.64 percent accuracy, 93.33 percent and 97.76 percent 5 and 10 fold cross-validation accuracy respectively. It is a computationally simple model which can be applied in real time identification tasks.

title: Hybrid Mode of Operation for a Grid-Integrated Dual Voltage Source Inverter
authors: Nimitha Muraleedharan, Mahesh K. Mishra
abstract: With the growing integration of renewable energy sources (RES)-fed voltage source inverters (VSIs), ensuring stable and reliable operation of the microgrid system, both in grid-connected and islanded modes, is essential to ensure uninterrupted power supply to loads. This necessitates a robust control strategy capable of handling dynamic transitions and grid disturbances. In this work, a hybrid mode of operation is presented for the dual voltage source inverter (DVSI) topology consisting of a parallel structure of two VSIs that provide active and reactive power to the load independently. The RES-fed VSI operates in grid-forming (GFM) mode, and the second inverter acts as a compensator in grid-following (GFL) mode. The GFM inverter regulates the system voltage and frequency, while the GFL VSI operation is dependent on its connection to the GFM inverter. The system also enables low voltage ridethrough (LVRT) by providing reactive power support to the grid. The DVSI system can be operated under different operating conditions, such as grid-connected, islanded, and weak or faulted grids, ensuring voltage and frequency regulation, load power continuity, and power quality of the system. The developed control strategy for the DVSI system is validated through simulation in MATLAB/Simulink under various disturbances.

title: Spatio-Temporal Coupled Coordinated Control of Electric Vehicles and Central HVAC Loads in Distribution Networks
authors: Yiming Chang, Qiaochu Zhao, Huan Pang, Yuan Wang, Yi Xu
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Electrical Automation and Artificial Intelligence (ICEAAI)
abstract: With the rapid growth of electric vehicles (EVs) and the increasing share of central air-conditioning (HVAC) demand in distribution systems, peak-load mitigation based on a single resource often triggers adverse effects such as synchronized charging spikes or comfort rebound. This paper addresses the coordinated control of EV charging/discharging and HVAC loads under spatio-temporal coupling constraints. First, we formulate a unified modeling framework in which EV battery dynamics and HVAC thermal inertia are embedded into a distribution-network-aware spatio-temporal coupling model. Second, a quantifiable coordination potential assessment method is proposed to characterize the feasible regulation boundaries and availability across nodes and time intervals. Third, we develop a multi-objective coordinated optimization strategy that jointly considers operational economy, user comfort, and distribution network security, and further present an engineering-oriented rolling control scheme. The proposed framework provides a reproducible modeling and optimization paradigm for coordinated demand-side flexibility at community/campus scales.

title: The Role of Small Language Models in Edge Computing and On-Device AI: A Review
authors: Jersho Shafin D J, Lankesh M, Allin Geo Vargheese
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: Small Language Models (SLMs) have surfaced as effective and efficient substitutes for Large Language Models, particularly in low-power, memory-limited, and privacyconscious settings. This paper offers a thorough examination of the architectures, optimization strategies, and deployment methodologies that facilitate high-performance on-device and edge-based inference utilizing SLMs. Contemporary encoder- and decoder-based SLMs-including DistilBERT, TinyBERT, MobileBERT, MiniLM, Qwen2, and Llama-3 8B-are analyzed with an emphasis on their architectural design decisions, compression techniques, and computational trade-offs. Essential optimization approaches such as pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation are investigated, along with deployment-specific improvements including operator fusion, hardware-aware tuning, and adaptive model scaling. Practical deployment workflows for edge devices like the Raspberry Pi 5 are also outlined to illustrate real-world applicability. Additionally, the paper underscores significant areas where SLMs are transforming edge intelligence, including on-device voice assistants, customer support systems, predictive text engines, industrial IoT analytics, and healthcare diagnostics. The findings indicate that SLMs provide low-latency, privacy-preserving, and energy-efficient AI functionalities, underscoring their importance in the forthcoming generation of intelligent edge systems.

title: A Computer Vision Based Approach for Real Time Classification and Placement
authors: Vishwa Priya V, Ladson Samuel Newton, Hariharan S, Kavishvar S, Niranjan R
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: This paper explains a ball sorting system that uses computer vision and a robot arm. A YOLOv8 model is trained to detect red, green, and blue balls. The model is tested with images and a live camera feed to make sure it works in real time. A conveyor belt moves the balls to a fixed pickup point. A camera above the conveyor captures the image of the ball. The YOLOv8 model identifies the color of the ball. The system then sends commands to a robot arm in RoboDK. The robot picks the ball from the pickup point and places it into the basket that matches its color. The system is made using Python, YOLOv8, OpenCV, and the RoboDK API. The results show that the robot can detect and sort balls correctly.

title: Design and Analysis of a Linear Induction Motor Based Syringe Pump for High-Precision Biomedical Applications
authors: Raju T, Sharmili Das
abstract: The precise and pulsation-free actuation of syringe pumps is a critical requirement in modern biomedical instrumentation. To meet this demand, this paper explores the use of a Linear Induction Motor (LIM) as a direct-drive solution, which avoids the mechanical backlash and jitter of traditional gear systems. We present the detailed design and analysis of a 2pole, single-sided LIM with a single-layer winding. A 3D model was constructed and simulated in ANSYS Maxwell to perform a transient analysis under three-phase voltage excitation. The study investigates critical performance metrics such as the magnitude and stability of the thrust force, induced secondary effects, and the overall magnetic field distribution. The finite element analysis demonstrates that the proposed LIM design meets the stringent requirements for smooth and precise motion, making it a viable and effective actuator for next-generation medical infusion systems.

title: Tutor Impact Analysis Using Rough Set Theory in Personalized Learning Platforms
authors: Shoira Usmanova, Shahlo Ahmedova, Jayrona Sultanova, Muhabbat Jumaniyozova, Gulchekhrakhon Mamasoliyeva, Azizbek Sobirov
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
abstract: Personalized learning platforms have surfaced as a powerful resource for increasing student engagement and personalizing instruction. Tutors' actions are one of many factors that have been recognized as important in the mediation of learning and outcomes. Unfortunately, aforementioned research has struggled to evidently model the impact of the tutor due to overlapping attributes, ambiguous and uncertain learner behaviours, and imprecise evaluative criteria. As such, the reported conclusions and recommendations are restricted from data-driven understanding about the contribution of tutor interventions to learner movement. In response to this problem, the paper presents the Rough Set-Based Tutor Influence Evaluation Model (RS-TIEM) to systematically address uncertainty employing Rough Set Theory (RST) while identifying tutor features that are important in terms of student performance. RS-TIEM has used indiscernibility relations, created lower approximations and upper approximations, and formed decision rules to show certain and possible dependencies between tutor actions and the success of the learner. These tutor actions include factors such as feedback timeliness, explanation clarity, and responsive adjustability, which all evoke a positive contribution to learning outcome success, and thus should contribute to a description and subsequent RS-TIEM evaluation. Empirical evaluation has shown that RS-TIEM outperforms conventional methods in interpretive robustness, accuracy, and transparency, and ensures trustworthiness in monitoring tutor performance. The proposed model has been effectively utilized in personalized learning environments, enhancing tutor assignment and adaptive learning processes.

title: Gamification in Online Learning: Evaluating Engagement and Classroom Performance in Higher Education
authors: Akanksha Upadhyaya, Deepika, Disha Garg
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Communication and Computers (ELEXCOM)
abstract: Gamification has gained significant traction as an innovative approach to enhance online learning, particularly in higher education. This study investigates the impact of gamification elements—such as points, badges, and leaderboards—on engagement, motivation, and academic performance among online learners. Drawing on data collected from 419 students representing diverse demographics and academic backgrounds, multiple linear regression analysis reveals that perceived enjoyment, motivation, and engagement are the strongest predictors of perceived classroom performance, while factors like satisfaction, ease of use, and self-efficacy also contribute positively. Notably, the sense of competition was not found to have a significant effect. The literature review and theoretical framework further validate the selection of these constructs, referencing established models such as the Gamification Acceptance Model (GAM), Self-Determination Theory (SDT), and the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM). The outcomes suggest that thoughtfully designed gamified elements can enhance both the effectiveness and satisfaction of online learning, with implications for increased motivation, learning achievement, and student satisfaction. However, individual learner differences and novelty effects should be carefully considered for sustained impact.

title: Implementation of System Identification for an EV Powered with Renewable Sources
authors: Gondu Vykunta Rao, Sudha Radhika
abstract: In the current industrial era, electric vehicle sales and technologies have become a significant area of interest for the commoner and industrialists, which has created a new scope for novel ideas in every sector of EVs. This work proposes a new methodology to meet the needs of academics and industries. The proposed work aims for the modelling and system identification (S.I) of a renewable-powered hybrid vehicle using a transfer function approach rather than current optimisation techniques because of the computation time and complexity in implementation for central sub system of the vehicle, like motor and fuel cell (FC), which further integrated with other subsystems of the vehicle sources as battery and super capacitor. The paper introduces traditional methods of nonlinear system identification, then implementation tendency and prospects of S.I., and validation in MATLAB, along with real-time hardware.

title: PFHFF: Parameter-Friendly Hierarchical Feature Fusion Module for Enhanced Epicardium Segmentation in CT Images
authors: Ibuki Naka, Jain Rahul Kumar, Yin-hao Li, Xianhua Han, Atsuyuki Wada, Atsunori Kashiwagi, Yutaro Iwamoto, Kiyosumi Maeda, Yen-Wei Chen, Yuji Tezuka
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Consumer Electronics (ICCE)
abstract: Epicardial adipose tissue (EAT) is strongly correlated with diabetes risk, but its diagnosis is easily affected by external factors such as diet. CT-based quantification of EAT requires accurate epicardium segmentation, which remains challenging due to low contrast and thin boundaries. Conventional CNN-based models like DenseUNet effectively capture local details but fail to model global context, leading to over-extraction and irregular contours. To address this, we propose a dual-encoder network combining MedViT and CNN, integrated through a novel Parameter-Friendly Hierarchical Feature Fusion (PFHFF) module. PFHFF aligns multi-scale features from both encoders and fuses them using element-wise multiplication, functioning as a lightweight gating mechanism to emphasize regions jointly activated by global and local features. This design enhances boundary delineation while maintaining computational efficiency. Experiments show that our method achieves smoother contours closer to the ground truth and improves F1 score by over 3.5% and IoU by 2.4% compared to conventional approaches.

title: Daily Mutual Fund Return Prediction using Optimized Stacked LSTM
authors: Rishita Das, Tapas Si
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Communication and Computers (ELEXCOM)
abstract: Mutual fund performance prediction remains challenging due to complex, non-linear market fluctuations that traditional machine learning regression models cannot effectively encompass. While tree-based models like Random Forest (RF) and XGBoost show promise in financial forecasting, they lack the capability to model temporal dependencies inherent in financial time series data. This study presents the first application of optimized Stacked Long Short-Term Memory (SLSTM) neural networks for predicting daily mutual fund returns in India. Using comprehensive data from SBI BlueChip Fund (January 2016 to December 2024, 3,289 observations), we implemented a 2-layer SLSTM architecture with optimized hyperparameters, validated through 10-fold cross-validation. Performance of the models is evaluated using six well-known metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), Coefficient of Determination or R-Squared (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>), and Adjusted R-squared $\left( {R_{adj}^2} \right)$. Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) using TOPSIS method has been conducted using these aforementioned metrics, resulting SLSTM as the best model and SLSTM achieved mean values of 0.6691 MAE, 2.7991 MAPE, 0.9857 RMSE, 0.9798 MSE, 0.0335 R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, and 0.0031 $R_{adj}^2$. This research constitutes the first systematic application of SLSTM for Indian mutual fund analysis, demonstrating that temporal modeling outperforms traditional methods during market volatility, providing crucial insights for fund managers and investors.

title: Quantitative Analysis of Operations & Maintenance Outsourcing Challenges in Thermal Power Plants: A Comprehensive Statistical Investigation Based on Industry Survey
authors: Aseemkumar Joshi, Abhishek Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Communication and Computers (ELEXCOM)
abstract: Operations and Maintenance (O&M) outsourcing in thermal power plants presents complex multifaceted challenges affecting operational efficiency, cost optimization, and stakeholder satisfaction. This comprehensive study presents quantitative analysis based on a structured 20-question survey administered to 45 Heads of Departments across 12 thermal power facilities representing 8,400 MW cumulative capacity in India. The research employs advanced statistical methodologies including exploratory factor analysis, multiple regression modeling, cluster analysis, and structural equation modeling to identify critical challenge factors and their interdependencies. Key findings reveal that remote location challenges, as identified through factor analysis, constitute the primary factor explaining 67.3% of variance among geographic and infrastructure-related outsourcing difficulties with statistical significance (p < 0.001). Community relations issues directly influence 58.9% of respondents’ operational decision-making processes, as measured through Likert-scale assessments, and Productivity Linked Performance Based Contracts (PLPBC) implementation demonstrates 43.2% higher implementation complexity scores (M = 4.23, SD = 0.68) compared to traditional manpower contracts (M = 2.94, SD = 0.57), representing a large effect size (Cohen’s d = 2.05). Reliability analysis confirms survey instrument validity (Cronbach’s alpha = 0.847), while principal component analysis identifies four primary challenge clusters explaining 82.4% of total variance. Multiple regression modeling achieves R-square = 0.789 in predicting outsourcing success outcomes. The study provides empirical foundation for evidence-based decision-making in thermal power plant O&M outsourcing through comprehensive quantitative insights derived from industry practitioners’ experiences.

title: TCAD-Based Investigation of a Wavelength-Tunable Near-Infrared Photodetector Using Vertical TFET with Optical Gate
authors: Pasumala Vamshidhar Reddy, Raushan Kumar, Kallepelli Sagar, Anil Kumar Pathakamuri
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Communication and Computers (ELEXCOM)
abstract: This paper presents a novel Near-Infrared Gated Vertical Tunnel Field-Effect Transistor (NIRG-VTFET) photodetector designed for high sensitivity and spectral selectivity across varying NIR wavelengths. The proposed device employs a vertically aligned silicon optical gate illuminated by incident NIR light, where photogenerated carriers modulate the tunneling barrier at the source–channel interface, enabling optical control over the tunneling process. Device characteristics are comprehensively analyzed using Sentaurus TCAD simulations by incorporating optical generation, non-local band-to-band tunneling, and quantum confinement models. The simulation results reveal that a decrease in incident wavelength from 1050 nm to 750 nm significantly enhances the photogeneration rate, optical potential, ON-current, sensitivity, and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), with peak sensitivity observed at 750 nm. These characteristics highlight the device’s potential for high-performance, low-power photodetection in NIR imaging, biomedical sensing, and optoelectronic communication.

title: Theoretical Modelling of Kretschmann SPR sensor to Analyse Refractive Index Shifts from Healthy to Cancer Cell
authors: Akash Srivastava, Sajal Agarwal, Sandhir Kumar Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, Communication and Computers (ELEXCOM)
abstract: The present study investigates a multilayered surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor based on the Kretschmann configuration, aimed at detecting and distinguishing various cancer cell types through changes in refractive index (RI). The analysis covers biological samples ranging from normal cells (RI=1.350) to highly malignant glioblastoma cells (RI = 1.447), evaluating critical performance metrics such as resonance angle, full width at half maximum (FWHM), sensitivity, figure of merit (FoM), and penetration depth (PD). Simulation results demonstrate a consistent increase in the SPR angle with rising RI, yielding an average sensitivity of 86.23°/RIU and peaking at 97.02°/RIU during the transition from glioma to glioblastoma. The incorporation of Tungsten Ditelluride (WTe<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>) and graphene significantly enhances electric field confinement and analyte interaction, resulting in improved FoM and sensing performance. Additionally, a comparative study between conventional SPR and long-range SPR (LRSPR) configurations indicates that LRSPR offers substantially deeper field penetration (~995.20 nm) and a narrower resonance width (FWHM = 0.43°), making it especially suitable for whole-cell detection.

title: Research and Development of a Fully Automated Testing Assembly Line for Electric Energy Meters and Collection Terminals
authors: Wenjun Zhu, Yuyun Zhang, Jiajun Feng
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Power, Electronics and Green Energy (ICPEGE)
abstract: This article designs and develops a full performance automated testing assembly line for electric energy meters and collection terminals, achieving automated testing and full process management of electric energy meters, intelligent IoT electric energy meters, concentrators, specialized transformer terminals, and secondary circuit inspection instruments for full performance testing. The automated testing assembly line has functions such as automatic sample grabbing, automatic flow, automatic testing, anomaly monitoring, and test management. The main functional modules of the detection system include automatic grasping unit, conveying unit, appearance testing unit, electromagnetic compatibility testing unit, multifunctional testing unit, anomaly monitoring unit, and intelligent control unit. Design and development of automated detection assembly line to enhance the detection capabilities of electric energy meters and collection terminals, ensuring the supply quality of measurement and collection equipment.

title: Error Analysis of the Normalized Sine-Wave Histogram Test Algorithm for ADC Linearity Test
authors: Cheng Ban, Jia Shen, Minshun Wu, Jiangtao Xu, Li Geng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The sine-wave histogram test (SHT) is a prevalent method for characterizing the static linearity of analog-to-digital converters (ADCs). The normalized SHT (NSHT) algorithm has gained wide adoption in histogram data processing owing to its conceptual simplicity and intuitive extension from the classic ramp histogram test (RHT). However, this paper reveals a fundamental and long-overlooked algorithmic error inherent in the NSHT, which degrades the accuracy of integral nonlinearity (INL) estimation. This error originates from the normalization process, where the measured non-uniform histogram is divided by an ideal sine-wave distribution to estimate code bin widths. This process erroneously assumes a proportional relationship between histogram count and code bin width. This assumption holds only for linear stimuli or ideal ADCs in the SHT. Consequently, estimation errors are introduced in code bin widths, systematically accumulated in code transition levels, and ultimately propagated to differential nonlinearity (DNL) and INL. Critically, since this error does not scale linearly with the ADC intrinsic nonlinearity, it persists even after applying standard static gain and offset correction. To isolate this error, the IEEE standard SHT (SSHT) algorithm and the RHT are employed as reference methods in our comparative analysis. Simulation and experimental results show that although the NSHT yields a DNL estimation error on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-3</sup> LSB, its accumulated INL error reaches −1.99 LSB, a magnitude that is 1.27 times the reference INL. Therefore, this paper exposes a critical flaw in the NSHT, and calls for a re-evaluation of any normalized histogram-based test that employs nonlinear stimuli.

title: An Underwater Target Tracking Method with Non-Gaussian Measurement Noises based on SGαS Distribution Modeling
authors: Chaofeng Lan, Rui Guo, Huan Chen, Jinghan Wang, Jintao Yang, Shilong Zhao, Yingqi Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In underwater target tracking, complex environmental interference and sensor anomalies introduce outliers into observations, leading to suboptimal state estimation. To address this challenge, a robust Kalman filter (SGαSSWMRKF) based on sliding window median and Sub-Gaussian α-stable (SGαS) distribution is proposed. The SGαS distribution models measurement noise in underwater tracking, with the Inverse Wishart distribution as the prior for measurement noise covariance. For the lack of a closed-form expression of the SGαS mixture probability density function, an estimator integrating Gamma Series approximation and Gauss-Laguerre Quadrature is developed to adaptively update asymptotic residual variance. Additionally, a median-based adaptive sliding window method with dynamically adjusted size computes the smoothed target state posterior, balancing real-time performance and estimation accuracy. Leveraging the Variational Bayes framework, SGαSSWMRKF suppresses outlier interference by iteratively updating the target state, measurement noise covariance, auxiliary random variables, and degrees of freedom parameters. Simulation results demonstrate that under an unknown non-stationary HMN environment, the SGαSSWMRKF outperforms the SWRKF filter. Specifically, it reduces the Average Root Mean Square Error by 16.74%, 8.14%, and 7.19% in distance, bearing, and velocity, respectively. Consequently, more accurate state estimation is achieved with SGαSSWMRKF. Additionally, experiments on a publicly available real-world dataset demonstrate that SGαSSWMRKF enables more accurate state estimation.

title: A Fast Phaseless Inversion Method With Characteristic Modes for Near-Field Measurements
authors: Zheng He, Kai Huang, Lixiao Wang, Han Lin, Richard Xian-Ke Gao, Qing Huo Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper proposes a fast phaseless inversion method based on characteristic mode analysis (CMA) for nearfield measurement systems. Targeting applications such as antenna characterization and EMC diagnostics, the method reconstructs mode currents on an equivalent surface by combining surface integral equations with characteristic mode solutions. It replaces the basis functions used in traditional techniques, significantly reducing the number of unknowns. Crucially, by utilizing magnitude-only data, this approach eliminates the need for complex phase calibration, thereby enhancing measurement robustness and quality in practical environments. To validate the effectiveness, near-field magnitude data are collected from simulations of a filtering magneto-electric dipole antenna, a dualpolarized dipole filtenna, and a planar inverted-F antenna, as well as measurements of a patch antenna and a patch antenna array. The results show that the proposed method is approximately 100 times faster than traditional methods with a relative data error below 1%, demonstrating its potential for high-efficiency and calibration-free radiation prediction.

title: Rack-Aware MSR Codes with Linear Field Size and Smaller Sub-Packetization for Tolerating Multiple Erasures
authors: Hengming Zhao, Dianhua Wu, Minquan Cheng
abstract: In an (n, k, d) rack-aware storage model, the system consists of n nodes uniformly distributed across n successive racks, such that each rack contains u nodes of equal capacity and the reconstructive degree satisfies k = ku + v where k = ⌊k/u⌋, 0 ≤ v ≤ u − 1. Suppose there are h ≥ 1 failed nodes in a rack (called the host rack). Then together with its surviving nodes, the host rack downloads recovery data from d helper racks and repairs its failed nodes. In this paper, we focus on the rack-aware minimum storage regenerating (MSR) codes for repairing h failed nodes within the same rack. By using the coupled-layer construction with the alignment technique, we construct a class of rack-aware MSR codes for all k+1 ≤ d ≤ n−1 which achieve the small sub-packetization l = s⌈n/s⌉ where the field size q increases linearly with n and s = d − k + 1. In addition, these codes achieve the optimal repair bandwidth for 1 ≤ h ≤ u−v, and the asymptotically optimal repair bandwidth for u − v + 1 ≤ h ≤ u. In particular, they achieve the optimal access when h = u−v. It is worth noting that the existing rack-aware MSR codes which achieve the same sub-packetization l = s⌈n/s⌉ are only known for the special case of d = n − 1, h = 1, and the field size is much larger than ours.

title: New Associate Editor
authors: Boris Murmann

title: CTSNet: Cross-Modal Token Selection Network for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
authors: Yi'ao Jia, Yurong Qian, Jun Long, Yuanxu Wang, Sen Luo, Dongbin Hu, Weijun Gong
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The fusion of low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) and high-spatial-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI) aims to combine the advantages of both modalities to enhance both spatial and spectral resolution, thereby generating high-spatial-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI). However, existing methods still struggle to balance global-local feature modeling and computational efficiency, and they face a core challenge: spectral distortion during the upsampling due to the lack of cross-modal guidance. To address these issues, this paper proposes a cross-modal token selection network, termed CTSNet. A novel cross-modal guided spatial implicit upsampling pyramid (SIUP) structure is introduced. Unlike conventional implicit neural representation (INR) methods, SIUP directly incorporates MSI features as conditional inputs during the local Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) prediction stage, providing precise spatial priors to the HSI upsampling process. This design enables early-stage and deep fusion of cross-modal information, effectively resolving the problems of spatial detail blurring and spectral distortion during upsampling. Secondly, a token selection Transformer block (TSTB) is proposed to collaboratively extract global-local spatial and spectral features through a parallel dual-branch structure. A token selection attention mechanism (TSAM) is further introduced to significantly reduce computational complexity by employing an adjustable token selection rate. Finally, we design a multi-scale hybrid fusion (MSHF) module to achieve deep feature reconstruction. Experiments on four public hyperspectral datasets demonstrate that CTSNet outperforms current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

title: RWKV-CD: A Vision-RWKV Framework for Building Change Detection From Remotely Sensed Imagery
authors: Chunyang Liu, Yating Pan, Tianyang Chen, Chao Liu, Shenghua Xu, Bo Mao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Buildings serve as a key indicator of human land use, and building change detection plays a crucial role in urban growth and shrinkage monitoring, land use planning, and urban development assessment and management. Current mainstream change detection methods mainly rely on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Transformer, and their variants. Although CNN excel at extracting local image features, their fixed and limited receptive fields hinder effective capture of long-range dependencies along complex building boundaries. Transformer, on the other hand, leverage self-attention mechanisms to effectively capture global information but suffer from quadratic computational complexity, leading to high demand of computing resources and limiting their practical efficiency. Meanwhile, since the spectra of edge pixels often represent a mixture of building and surrounding surfaces (e.g., roads or vegetation), existing methods typically overlook the variations in spectral similarity between the main body and its edges. To address this challenge, we leverage the Receptance Weighted Key Value (RWKV) architecture for its ability to capture and propagate global dependencies with linear complexity. Building upon this foundation, we propose the RWKV-CD, a novel change detection method that integrates RWKV architecture with specialized feature extraction and noise suppression strategies. RWKV-CD employs a dual-branch encoder separately capture global building information and fine-grained edge details, while a cross-attention module adaptively fuse these features.
